question:
Read the ${\rm{Assertion}}$ and ${\rm{Reason}}$ carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: When an iron cylinder is placed on top of a wooden block on a soft horizontal floor causing both to accelerate downwards, the force exerted by the wooden block on the floor decreases

Reason: The force exerted by the wooden block on the floor is equal to the difference between the gravitational force on the system and the normal force exerted by the floor, which decreases due to the acceleration.
explanation: Both the Assertion and the Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion. The normal force exerted by the floor decreases because it must counteract not only the weight of the system (wooden block plus iron cylinder) but also the downward acceleration of the system. Hence, the force exerted by the block on the floor is the normal force, which is less than the weight of the system due to acceleration, resulting in a decrease. Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question:
Read the ${\rm{Assertion}}$ and ${\rm{Reason}}$ carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: Tension in a string is a type of contact force that arises due to the elasticity of the string.

Reason: The tension in a string is actually a manifestation of the electromagnetic forces at the molecular level within the material of the string.
explanation: While both the assertion and reason are true, the reason does not directly explain why tension is considered a contact force. The reason is discussing the fundamental origin of the tension force. Correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

question: A system as shown in the figure is in equilibrium and is at rest. The spring and string are massless, now the string is cut. The acceleration of the masses $2 m$ and $m$ just after the string is cut, will be
explanation:
Given, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952241/preptracker/questions/q_107608_explanation_1764952240547.png When string 1 is cut, $m_{2}$ undergoes free fall.
$\therefore$ Acceleration of $m_{2}$ be $a_{2}=g$, down wards Just after the string is cut, resultant upward force on $m_{1}$
$=3 m g-2 m g=m g$.
$\therefore$ Acceleration of $m_{1}$,
$a_{1}=\dfrac{m g}{2 m}=\dfrac{g}{2}$, upwards.
So, the correct option is (1).

question: Two blocks of masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ are suspended from the ends of a light string passing over a smooth fixed pulley. If the pulley is pulled up with an acceleration $g$, then the tension in the string will be
explanation:
Considering $m_{2}>m_{1}$, forces acting on the blocks are shown in the figure. The pulley is made to move upward with acceleration $a=g$
Let $f$ be the acceleration of the blocks relative to the pulley centre $O . m_{1} a$ and $m_{2} a$are pseudo forces acting on the blocks when observed from pulley centre. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952231/preptracker/questions/q_107603_explanation_1764952230638.png For $m_{2}, m_{2} g+m_{2} a-T=m_{2} f$
$\, \Rightarrow 2{m_2}g - T = {m_2}f\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
For $m_{1}, T-m_{1} g-m_{1} a=m_{1} f$
$ \Rightarrow T - 2{m_1}g = {m_1}f\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Dividing (1) by (2),
$\dfrac{2 m_{2} g-T}{T-2 m_{1} g}=\dfrac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}$
$\Rightarrow 2 m_{1} m_{2} g-m_{1} T=T \cdot m_{2}-2 m_{1} m_{2} g$
$\Rightarrow T=\left(\dfrac{4 m_{1} m_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}\right) g$.
So the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: If a man of mass $50\,kg$ is in a lift moving down with an acceleration equal to acceleration due to gravity, then the apparent weight of the man is
explanation:
Given,
mass of man $m=50 {~kg}$
Acceleration due to gravity $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957837/preptracker/questions/q_107602_explanation_1764957837126.png Pseudo force (upward) acting on the
man $=m a=m g$.
$N=$ Normal reaction of weighing machine.
$\therefore N+m a=m g \Rightarrow N=m g-m a=0$
$\therefore$ Apparent weight $=0$
So, the correct option is (1).

question: A man weighing $75\,kg$ is standing in a lift. The weight of the man standing on a weighing machine kept in the lift when the lift is moving downwards freely under gravity is
explanation:
Given, mass of man $m=75 {~kg}$
Lift is going downward with acceleration
$a=g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957836/preptracker/questions/q_107601_explanation_1764957835682.png Pseudo force (upward) acting on the
$\operatorname{man}=m a=m g$.
$N=$ Normal reaction of weighing machine.
$\therefore N+m a=m g \Rightarrow N=m g-m g=0$
So, the correct option is (1).

question: A constant horizontal force $F$ of magnitude $10 N$ is applied to a block $A$ and this produces an acceleration of magnitude $20 {~m} / {s}^{2}$. If this block is then kept against another block of mass $1.5\,kg$ as shown in figure and a force $F^{\prime}$ of $20 N$ is applied, find the force on the block $B$. (Neglect friction)
explanation:
Force applied on block $A, F=10 N$
Acceleration produced in block $A$, $a=20 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
According to Newton's second equation of motion,
$F=m_{A} a \Rightarrow m_{A}=\dfrac{F}{a}=\dfrac{10}{20}=0.5 {~kg}$
Again, block $A$ is kept in contact with block $B$, where $m_{B}=1.5 {~kg}$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957828/preptracker/questions/q_107596_explanation_1764957827470.png If $a_{1}$ be the common acceleration in the block system and $R$ be reaction force on block $A$ and $B$, then from free body diagram of block $A$ and $B$,
Equation of motion of block $A$ is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957829/preptracker/questions/q_107596_explanation_1764957828676.png ${F^\prime } - R = {m_A} \cdot {a_1} \Rightarrow 20 - R = 0.5{a_1}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
For block $B, \quad R=m_{B} a_{1}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957829/preptracker/questions/q_107596_explanation_1764957829387.png $ \Rightarrow R = 1.5{a_1}{\text{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we have
$20-1.5 a_{1}=0.5 a_{1} \Rightarrow 20=2 a_{1}$
$\Rightarrow a_{1}=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
From Eqs. (2), $R=1.5 a_{1}=1.5 \times 10=15 {~N}$
Hence, force on block $B$ will be $15 N$.
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Multiple Blocks in Contact

question: At time $t=0$, a force $F=\alpha t$, where $t$ is time in seconds, is applied to a body of mass $1\,kg$, resting on a smooth horizontal plane. If the direction of the force makes an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal, then the velocity of the body at the moment of its breaking off the plane is
explanation:
The given situation is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952250/preptracker/questions/q_107595_explanation_1764952250272.png Body loses contact with surface when reaction is zero, thus $F \sin 45^{\circ}=m g$
$\Rightarrow F=\dfrac{m g}{\sin 45^{\circ}}=\dfrac{1 \times 10}{\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}} \Rightarrow F=10 \sqrt{2} {~N}$
But, $F=\alpha t$
Hence, $\alpha t=10 \sqrt{2} \Rightarrow t=\dfrac{10 \sqrt{2}}{\alpha}$
Now, acceleration of body,
$a=\dfrac{F}{m} \cos 45^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{d v}{d t}=\dfrac{\alpha t}{m} \cos 45^{\circ}$
or $d v=\dfrac{\alpha}{\sqrt{2} \times 1} \times t \times d t$
$\Rightarrow \int_{0}^{v} d v=\dfrac{\alpha}{\sqrt{2}} \int_{0}^{\frac{10 \sqrt{2}}{\alpha}} t \cdot d t$
$\Rightarrow v-0=\dfrac{\alpha}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\dfrac{t^{2}}{2}\right)_{0}^{\frac{10 \sqrt{2}}{\alpha}}$
$\Rightarrow v=\dfrac{\alpha}{\sqrt{2}} \times \dfrac{100 \times 2}{2 \times \alpha^{2}}=\dfrac{50 \sqrt{2}}{\alpha} {~m} / {s}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Block / Multiple Blocks Connected by a String

question: Imagine a person standing on a weighing machine placed inside an elevator. The elevator first accelerates, then moves with a constant velocity and finally decelerates at constant rate to stop. The maximum and minimum weight recorded are $80\,kgf$ and $64\,kgf$, respectively. Find out the true weight of that person considering ( $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
When the lift accelerates with acceleration $a$, then the normal reaction force $N$ by the elevator on the person is given as https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957822/preptracker/questions/q_107583_explanation_1764957821780.png $\therefore N-m g=m a$ (where, $m$ is the mass of that person)
$ \Rightarrow N = m(g + a) = 80g{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
When the lift decelerates with acceleration $a$ then, $m g-N=m a$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957823/preptracker/questions/q_107583_explanation_1764957822535.png $ \Rightarrow N = m(g - a) = 64g\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
$\Rightarrow$ Dividing Eq. (2) by Eq. (1), we get
$\dfrac{g-a}{g+a}=\dfrac{64 g}{80 g} \Rightarrow \dfrac{g-a}{g+a}=\dfrac{4}{5}$
$\Rightarrow 5 g-5 a=4 g+4 a$
$g=9 a \Rightarrow a=\dfrac{g}{9}$
$\therefore$ From Eq. (1), we get,
$80 g=m(g+a)=m\left(g+\dfrac{g}{9}\right)$
$\Rightarrow 80 g=\dfrac{10 g}{9} m$
$\Rightarrow m=\dfrac{80 \times 9}{10}=72 {~kg}$
So, the true weight of that person is $72 {~kg} f$.
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: An infinite number of masses are placed on a frictionless table and they are connected via massless strings. Their masses follow the sequence, $m,\frac{m}{2},\frac{m}{6}, \ldots  \ldots  \ldots ..\frac{m}{{n!}}, \ldots  \ldots  \ldots $ and they are further connected to a mass $m$ that  hangs over a massless pulley. The acceleration  of the hanging mass is
explanation:
The given situation is shown in the following figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957817/preptracker/questions/q_107578_explanation_1764957816650.png Effective mass of the system of $n$ masses placed on the table,
$M=m+\dfrac{m}{2}+\dfrac{m}{6}+\ldots+\dfrac{m}{n!}$
$=m\left(1+\dfrac{1}{2}+\dfrac{1}{6}+\ldots+\dfrac{1}{n!}\right)$
$=m\left(\dfrac{1}{1!}+\dfrac{1}{2!}+\dfrac{1}{3!}+\ldots+\dfrac{1}{n!}\right)$
$=m\left(1+\dfrac{1}{1!}+\dfrac{1}{2!}+\dfrac{1}{3!}+\ldots+\dfrac{1}{n!}-1\right)$
$M=m(e-1)$
$\left[\because e=1+\dfrac{1}{1!}+\dfrac{1}{2!}+\dfrac{1}{3!}+\ldots+\dfrac{1}{n!}\right]$
The given system of masses can be redrawn as https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957817/preptracker/questions/q_107578_explanation_1764957817434.png If $a$ be the acceleration of system of masses, then according to Newton's second law of motion,
$T = Ma \Rightarrow T = m(e - 1) \times a{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
and $m g-T=m a \Rightarrow T=m g-m a$
$T = m(g - a)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eq. (1) and Eq. (2), we get$m(e-1) a=m(g-a) \Rightarrow a=g / e$
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Block / Multiple Blocks Connected by a String

question: Three blocks are connected by massless strings on a frictionless inclined plane of $30^{\circ}$ as shown in the figure. A force of $104\,N$ is applied upward along the incline to mass $m_{3}$ causing an upward motion of the blocks. What is the acceleration of the blocks? (Assume, acceleration due to gravity, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
The given situation is shown in the figure below, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952219/preptracker/questions/q_107568_explanation_1764952218906.png If the system moves upward with an acceleration $a\,m/{s^2}$ on the application of 104 $N$, then the resultant external unbalanced force on the system of three blocks
$=\left(104-8 g \sin 30^{\circ}-5 g \sin 30^{\circ}-3 g \sin 30^{\circ}\right) N$
$=\left(104-80 \times \dfrac{1}{2}-50 \times \dfrac{1}{2}-30 \times \dfrac{1}{2}\right) N$
$=24 {~N}$,
$\therefore By$ Newton's second law of motion
$(3+5+8) \times a=24 \Rightarrow a=1.5 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Block / Multiple Blocks Connected by a String

question:
Consider a system of blocks $X, A$ and $B$ as shown in the figure. The blocks $A$ and $B$ have equal mass and are connected by a massless string through a massless pulley. The coefficient of friction between block $A$ and $X$ or $B$ and $X$ is 0.5 . If block $X$ moves on the horizontal frictionless surface what should be its minimum acceleration such that blocks $A$ and $B$ remain stationary.
($g=$ acceleration due to gravity.)
explanation:
Given, $m_{A}=m_{B}=m$
Coefficient of friction between each block and surface, $\mu=0.5$.
From the frame of $X$, forces acting on $A$ and $B$ are as shown in the figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957806/preptracker/questions/q_107566_explanation_1764957806412.png For equilibrium of block $A$,
$T = \mu mg + ma\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
For equilibrium of block $B$,
$T + \mu ma = mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Solving, $a=\dfrac{g(1-\mu)}{(1+\mu)}=\dfrac{g(1-0.5)}{(1+0.5)} {m} / {s}^{2}=\dfrac{g}{3} {~m} / {s}^{2}$.
So the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: A bar of mass $m$ resting on a smooth horizontal plane starts moving due to force $|F|=\dfrac{m g}{9}$. The magnitude of the force remains constant with time. The force vector makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal which varies with the distance covered as $\theta=C x$. If the constant, $C=10\left(\dfrac{\text { degree }}{\text { meter }}\right)$, then the speed of the bar, when angle $\theta$ becomes equal to $30^{\circ}$ for the first time, is (Take, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
Figure shows a force acting on a bar. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952210/preptracker/questions/q_107565_explanation_1764952210364.png Given, $F = \frac{{mg}}{9}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
So, the horizontal component of force acting on the bar
$F_{H}=F \cos \theta=F \cos 10 x$
Here, $\theta=c x$ and $c=10^{\circ} {m}^{-1}$.
Hence, at an angle $30^{\circ}$,
$\theta = 10x \Rightarrow 30^\circ = 10x \Rightarrow x = 3\,m\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From, energy conservation law,
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\left|\int F \cdot \cos \theta d x\right|$
Substituting the values $F$ from Eq. (1), we get
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{m g}{9}\left|\int_{0}^{3} \cos 10 x d x\right|$
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{10}{9} m \times \dfrac{\sin 30^{\circ}}{10}$
$\Rightarrow v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{9} \Rightarrow v=0.33 {~m} / {s}$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Applications of Newton’s Laws

question: A block of mass $5\,kg$ is kept against an accelerating wedge with a wedge angle of $45^{\circ}$ to the horizontal. The coefficient of friction between the block and the wedge is $\mu=0.4$. What is the minimum absolute value of the acceleration of the wedge to keep the block steady? [Assume, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ ]
explanation:
Let $a=$ acceleration of wedge. Then pseudo force on block due to acceleration of wedge $=m a$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957803/preptracker/questions/q_107559_explanation_1764957803345.png Then, from FBD we have,
For equilibrium of block, $m g \sin \alpha=m a \cos \alpha+\mu(m g \cos \alpha+m a \sin \alpha)$
$\Rightarrow g=a+\mu(g+a) \Rightarrow \dfrac{0.6}{1.4} g=a$
$\left[\because \alpha=45^{\circ} \text { and } \mu=0.4\right]$
$\Rightarrow a=\dfrac{30}{7} m s^{-2}$
So,the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

explanation:

$a = \frac{{{\rm{ net }}\,{\rm{external}}\,{\rm{ force }}\,{\rm{along}}\,{\rm{ }}\vec a}}{{{\rm{ Total }}\,{\rm{mass }}}}\,$

question: A light unstretchable string passing over a smooth light pulley connects two blocks of masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$. If the acceleration of the system is $\dfrac{g}{8}$, then the ratio of the masses $\dfrac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951445/preptracker/questions/q_35378_explanation_1764951445525.png
From free body diagram $m_{2} g-T=m_{2} a$ $\quad \,$(1)
$T - {m_2}g = {m_2}a$ $\quad \,(2)$
From equation (1) and (2), we get
$\begin{aligned}& \left(m_{2}-m_{1}\right) g=\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) a \\& \text { or } \quad a=\dfrac{m_{2}-m_{1}}{m_{1}+m_{2}} g \Rightarrow \dfrac{g}{8}=\dfrac{\left(m_{2}-m_{1}\right) g}{m_{1}+m_{2}} \\& \text { or } m_{1}+m_{2}=8 m_{2}-8 m_{1} \\& \Rightarrow 9 m_{1}=7 m_{2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Atwood Machine & Pulley Related Problems

question: A light string passing over a smooth light pulley connects two blocks of masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ (where $m_{2}>m_{1}$ ). If the acceleration of the system is $\dfrac{g}{\sqrt{2}}$, then the ratio of the masses  $\dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}$ is
explanation:
From the $F B D$ of block of mass $m_{1}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955879/preptracker/questions/q_35371_explanation_1764955879100.png
$T-m_{1} g=m_{1} a$ $\quad \quad (1)$
From the $F B D$ of block of mass $m_{2}$,
${m_2}g - T = {m_2}a$ $\quad \quad (2)$
From equations (1) and (2) we have
$g\left(m_{2}-m_{1}\right)=a\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right)$
or $\quad g\left(m_{2}-m_{1}\right)=\dfrac{g}{\sqrt{2}}\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right)$
or $\sqrt{2}\left(m_{2}-m_{1}\right)=\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right)$
or $m_{2}(\sqrt{2}-1)=m_{1}(\sqrt{2}+1)$
or $\dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2}-1}{\sqrt{2}+1}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Atwood Machine & Pulley Related Problems

question: A wooden block of mass $5\,\,kg$ rests on a soft horizontal floor. When an iron cylinder of mass $25\,\,kg$ is placed on the top of the block, the floor yields steadily and the block and the cylinder together go down with an acceleration of   $0.1\,\,m{s^{ - 2}}$. The action force of the system on the floor is equal to
explanation:
Given, mass of wooden block, $m = 5\,kg$
Mass of cylinder, $M = 25\,kg$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956506/preptracker/questions/q_35363_explanation_1764956506317.png When iron cylinder is placed on block, then
$\begin{aligned}& (M+m) g-R=(M+m) a \\& (M+m) g-R=(M+m) \times 0.1 \\& \Rightarrow \quad 30 \times 9.8-R=30 \times 0.1 \\& \Rightarrow \quad R=291 N\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Applications of Newton’s Laws

question: Blocks $A$ and $B$ each have same mass $m=1 {~kg}$. Determine the largest horizontal force $P$ (in newton) which can be applied to $B$ so that $A$ will not slip up on $B$. Neglect any friction.
explanation:
If the block $A$ will not slip up on $B$, both the blocks should move together in horizontal direction, and the acceleration of the block $A$ in vertical direction should be zero
$P = (1 + 1)a = 2a\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955863/preptracker/questions/q_35254_explanation_1764955862835.png From $F.B.D.$ of the block $A$,
$N\cos 37^\circ = 1\,{\rm{g}}$
$N\sin 37^\circ = 1a$
From (2) and (3), $\dfrac{a}{g}=\tan 37^{\circ}$
$a=10 \times \dfrac{3}{4}=7.5 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
From (1), $P=2 \times 7.5=15 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: At $t=0$, the lower end of the bar $A$ is just above the upper end of bar $B$ (mass of bar $A=3 {~kg}$, mass of bar $B=\dfrac{11}{3} {~kg}$. Find time when upper end of block $A$ just crosses the lower end of $B$. (Assume the system was released at $t=0)$.$\left(g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}\right)$
explanation:
Acceleration of $A$ and $B=\left(\dfrac{\dfrac{11}{3}-3}{\dfrac{11}{3}+3}\right) g=1 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
Relative acceleration of $A$ and $B=2 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
Now by using $s=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2} ; 4=0+\dfrac{1}{2}(2)(t)^{2} \Rightarrow t=2 {~s}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Atwood Machine & Pulley Related Problems

question: Three blocks $A, B$ and $C$ of masses $10 {~kg}, 10 {~kg}$ and 20 $kg$ respectively are arranged as shown in figure. All the surfaces are frictionless and strings are inextensible. Pulleys are light. A constant force $F=20 {~N}$ is applied on block $A$ as shown. Pulleys and strings are light. Part of the strings connecting both pulleys is vertical and part of the strings connecting pulleys with blocks $A$ and $B$ are horizontal. Find the tension in the string.
explanation:
From $FBD$ it is clear no net force acts on the block $C$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951449/preptracker/questions/q_35238_explanation_1764951448721.png Hence the acceleration of block $C$ should be zero. The blocks $A$ and $B$ are connected directly by a string they will move with common acceleration (say $a$ ).
For block $B,{\rm{ }}T = 10a\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
For block $A,{\rm{ }}F - T = 10a\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) and (2), $a=\dfrac{F}{20}=\dfrac{20}{20}=1 {~ms}^{-2}$and $T=10 \times {I}=10 {~N}$
subject: Physics

question: In the figure shown, all pulleys are massless and frictionless. If the length of the rod $l=3.0 {~m}$, then find the time taken by the particle   to reach the upper end of the rod. (Take $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$).
explanation:
the constraint relation, ${a_2} = 2{a_1}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951420/preptracker/questions/q_35230_explanation_1764951420564.png
$T - mg = m{a_1}\quad \quad \,\,(1)$
$mg - \frac{T}{2} = m{a_2}\quad \quad (3)$
By solving (1), (2) and (3), we get
${a_1} = \left( {\frac{g}{5}} \right)m{s^2},\,\,{a_2} = \left( {\frac{{2g}}{5}} \right)m{s^2}$
${{\vec a}_{12}} = {{\vec a}_1} - {{\vec a}_2} = \frac{g}{5} - \left( { - \frac{{2g}}{5}} \right) = \frac{{3g}}{5}$
${{\vec s}_{12}} = {{\vec u}_{12}}\,t + \frac{1}{2}{{\vec a}_{12}}\,{t^2}$
${{\vec s}_{12}} = l$
${{\vec s}_{12}} = {{\vec u}_{12}}\,t + \frac{1}{2}{{\vec a}_{12}}\,{t^2}$
$l = 0 + \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{3}{5}g{t^2}$
$ \Rightarrow t = \sqrt {\frac{{10\,l}}{{3g}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{10 \times 3}}{{3 \times 10}}} = 1\,s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Atwood Machine & Pulley Related Problems

question: A man is standing in a lift which goes up and comes down with the same constant acceleration. If the ratio of the apparent weights in the two cases is $2: 1$, find the acceleration of the lift  (Take $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ ).
explanation:
When lift goes upwards with constant acceleration $a$
${W_1} = m(g + a)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
When lift goes downwards with constant acceleration $a$
${W_2} = m(g - a)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
$\frac{{{W_1}}}{{{W_2}}} = \frac{{m(g + a)}}{{m(g - a)}} = \frac{2}{1}$
$g + a = 2g - 2a$
$3a = g\,\,{\rm{ or, }}a = \frac{g}{3}$
$ = \frac{{10}}{3} = 3.33\,m/{s^2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: A block of weight ${W}$ produces an extension of $9\,cm$ when it is hung by an elastic spring of length $60\,cm$ and is in equilibrium. The spring is cut into two parts, one of length $40\,cm$ and the other of length $20\,cm$. The same load ${W}$ hangs in equilibrium supported by both parts as shown in the figure. Find the extension  in the spring.
explanation:
Let ${k}$ be the spring constant of the original spring ${W=9 k \Rightarrow k=\dfrac{W}{9}}$
Spring constant is inversely proportional to the length of the spring.
Spring constant for the $40\,cm$ piece ${k_{1}=k \times \dfrac{60}{40}=\dfrac{3}{2} k}$,
${k_{2}=k \times \dfrac{60}{20}=3 k}$
When connected in parallel, the equivalent spring constant ${k^{\prime}=k_{1}+k_{2} \Rightarrow k^{\prime}=\dfrac{3}{2} k+3 k=4 \dfrac{1}{2} k}$
Let ${x}$ ' be the new extension. Then ${k^{\prime} x^{\prime}=W=9 k}$
${\Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{9}{2} k \times x^{\prime}=9 k \Rightarrow x^{\prime}=9 k \times \dfrac{2}{9 k}=2 {~cm}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Block / Multiple Blocks Connected by a Spring

question: Two monkeys ${M_{1}}$ and ${M_{2}}$ of equal mass '${m}$' can climb strings of a pulley arrangement as shown in figure. Find magnitude of acceleration (in ${{m} / {s}^{2}}$) of ${M_{1}}$ with respect to rope so that block remains stationary. It is given that ${M_{2}}$ is just holding the string. Assume pulley is frictionless and string is massless and inextensible.
explanation:
${T=5 m g}$
${a_{2}=\dfrac{5 m g-m g}{m}=4 g}$
${T^{\prime}=2 T=10 {mg}}$
${a_{1}=\dfrac{10 {mg}-m g}{m}=9 g}$
Acceleration of the rope is equal to the acceleration of the moving pulley. Hence acceleration of ${M_{1}}$ with respect to rope https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955842/preptracker/questions/q_35082_explanation_1764955842014.png ${a_{{M_1},{\rm{ }}Rope{\rm{ }}}} = 9g - ( - 2g) = 11g = 110\,m/{s^2}$

question: In the system shown, block ${A}$ is of mass $4.0\,kg$ and blocks ${B}$ and ${C}$ are of equal mass each of $3\,kg$. Find the acceleration in  of block ${C}$, if the system is set free. ${\left[g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}\right]}$
explanation:
$F.B.D.$ of the blocks ${A, B}$ and ${C}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951408/preptracker/questions/q_35078_explanation_1764951407645.png Conditions of forces on ${B}$ and ${C}$ are same so they both will move with same downward acceleration ${a}$. The block ${A}$ moves with the same acceleration ${a}$ upwards
From block $A:2T - 40 = 4a\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
From block ${B}$ or $C:30 - T = 3a\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From above two equations ${a=2 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$

explanation:

${600-300=m(20)}$
${\dfrac{300}{20}=m}$

question: Figure shows a ball of mass ${m}$ connected with two ideal springs of force constant ${k}$, kept in equilibrium on a smooth incline, suddenly right spring is cut. What is magnitude of instantaneous acceleration (in ${{m} / {s}^{2}}$) of ball?
explanation:
Let initial extension in spring is ${x_{0}}$, then
$2 k x_{0} \cos 45^{\circ}=m g \sin 45^{\circ} \Rightarrow 2 k x_{0}=m g$
Let after cutting right spring, according of ball is ${A_{1}}$ along the inclined plane and ${a_{2}}$ perpendicular to ${a_{1}}$
${\Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{m g}{\sqrt{2}}-\dfrac{m g}{2} \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=m a_{1}}$
${\Rightarrow \quad a_{1}=\dfrac{g}{2 \sqrt{2}}}$
Then
${m g \sin 45^{\circ} \Rightarrow k x_{0} \cos 45^{\circ}=m a_{1}}$
$k x_{0} \sin 45^{\circ}=m a_{2} \Rightarrow a_{2}=\dfrac{g}{2 \sqrt{2}}$
Net acceleration ${a=\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}}=\dfrac{g}{2}=5 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Block / Multiple Blocks Connected by a Spring

question: A block of mass $15\,kg$ is placed at a distance of $10\,m$ from the rear end of a long trolley as shown in figure. The coefficient of friction between the block and the surface below is $0.4.$ Starting from rest, the trolley is given a uniform acceleration of $5\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}.$ At what distance (in metres) from the starting point will the block fall-off the trolley? (Take $g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$)
explanation:
Acceleration of the block $ = $ acceleration of the trolley $ = a = 5\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$
$\therefore$ The force acting on the block is,
$F = ma = 15 \times 5 = 75\,N$ in the backward direction.
The force of limiting friction $f$ is given by,$f = \mu mg = 0.4 \times 15 \times 10 = 60\,N$ in forward direction.
Hence, the net force on the block towards the right i.e., towards the rear end of the trolley is,$F' = F - f = 75 - 60 = 15\,N$
Acceleration due to this force is,
$a' = \frac{{F'}}{m} = \frac{{15}}{{15}} = 1\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$
Let $t$ be the time taken for the block to fall from the rear end of the trolley travelling a distance $s = 10\,m$
$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}{\rm{a}}{{\rm{t}}^2}$
$10 = 0 + \frac{1}{2} \times 1 \times {t^2}$
$\therefore t = \sqrt {20} \,s$
The distance covered by the trolley in time $t = \sqrt {20} \,s$ is given by,
$s' = ut + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2} = 0 + \frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 20$
$\therefore \quad s' = 50\,m$

question: The pulleys and strings shown in figure are smooth and of negligible mass. For the system to remain in equilibrium, the angle ${\theta}$ should be
explanation:
The free body diagram of the mass $\sqrt 2 m$, is shown in the figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955826/preptracker/questions/q_34899_explanation_1764955826255.png Balancing the force, we get
$2T\cos \theta = \sqrt 2 mg$
$ \Rightarrow 2\cos \theta = \sqrt 2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(\therefore T = mg)$
$ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} \Rightarrow \theta = {\rm{ }}45^\circ $.
So correct option is (1)

question: Three blocks of masses  ${m_1} = 2.0\,kg,$ ${m_2} = 4.0\,kg$ and ${m_3} = 6.0\,kg$ are connected by strings on a frictionless inclined plane of $60^{\circ}$, as shown in the figure. A force $F=120 N$ is applied upward along the incline to the uppermost block, causing an upward movement of the blocks. The connecting cords are light. The values of tensions $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ in the cords are
explanation:
The free body diagram are given below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956855/preptracker/questions/q_34838_explanation_1764956855414.png For the motion of mass $m_{1}$,
${T_1} - {m_1}g\cos 30^\circ = {m_1}a\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
For the motion of mass $m_{2}$,
${T_2} - {T_1} - {m_2}g\cos 30^\circ = {m_2}a{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
and for the motion of mass $m_{3}$,
$F - {T_2} - {m_3}g\cos 30^\circ = {m_3}a\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
Adding Eqs. (1), (2) and (3), we get
$F-\left(m_{1}+m_{2}+m_{3}\right) g \cos 30^{\circ}$
$=\left(m_{1}+m_{2}+m_{3}\right) a$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{F}{m_{1}+m_{2}+m_{3}}-g \cos 30^{\circ}=a$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{120}}{{12}} - 10 \times \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} = a$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,a = 10\left( {1 - \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right)$
Now, ${T_1} = {m_1}\left( {a + g\cos 30^\circ } \right)$
$ = 2\left( {10 - \frac{{10\sqrt 3 }}{2} + 10\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right) = 20\,N$
${T_2} = a\left( {{m_1} + {m_2}} \right) + \left( {{m_1} + {m_2}} \right)g\cos 30^\circ $
$ = 6 \times 10 = 60\,N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Block / Multiple Blocks Connected by a String

question: A $60\,kg$ man stands on a spring scale in the lift. At some instant he finds, scale reading has changed from $60\,kg$ to $50\,kg$ for a while and then comes back to the original make. What should we conclude?
explanation:
For upward accelerations, apparent
weight $ = m\,(g + a)$
If lift suddenly stops during upward motion,
then apparent weight $ = m\,(g - a)$ because instead
of acceleration, we will consider retardation.
In the problem, it is given that scale reading
initially was $60\,kg$ and due to sudden jerk reading
decreases and finally comes back to the original
mark, $i.\,e.$ $60\,kg.$
So, we can conclude that lift was moving
upwards with constant speed and suddenly stops.

options.text: The lift was in constant motion upwards.
The lift was in constant motion downwards.
The lift while in constant motion upwards, is suddenly stopped.
The lift while in constant motion downwards is suddenly stopped.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: A man of mass $60\,kg$ is riding in a lift. The weight of the man, when the lift is accelerating upwards and downwards at  $2\,m{s^{ - 2}},$  are respectively (take, $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ )
explanation:
When the lift is accelerating upwards with
a constant acceleration $a$, then the apparent
weight,
$w = m(g + a) = 60(10 + 2)$
$ = 60 \times 12 = 720\,N$
When the lift is accelerating
downwards at the rate $a$, then apparent weight,
${w^\prime } = m(g - a) = 60(10 - 2)$
$ = 60 \times 8 = 480\,N$

question: Two masses $10\,kg$ and  $20\,kg$ respectively are connected by a massless spring as shown in figure. A force of $200\,N$ acts on the $20\,kg$ mass. At the instant shown is figure, the $10\,kg$ mass has acceleration of  $12\,m/{s^2}.$  The value of acceleration of  $20\,kg$ mass is
explanation:
Equation of motion of mass ${m_1} = 10\,kg$ is
$F_{1}=m_{1} a_{1}=10 \times 12=120 \mathrm{~N}$
Force on $10\,kg$ mass is $120\,N$ to the right. As
action and reaction are equal and opposite, the
reactions force $F$ - on $20\,kg$ mass $F = 120\,N$ to
the left.
$\therefore$ Equation of motion of mass
${m_2} = 20\,kg$ is
$200 - F = 20{a_2}$
$200 - 120 = 20{a_2}$
$20{a_2} = 80$
${a_2} = \frac{{80}}{{20}} = 4\,m/{s^2}$

question: In a non-inertial frame, the second law of motion is written as
explanation:
In a non-inertial frame, the second law of
motion is written as $F=m a-F_{p}$ where, $F_{p}$ is
the pseudo force while $a$ is the acceleration of
the body relative to non-inertial frame.

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
An elevator can go up or down with uniform speed when its weight is balanced with the tension of its cable.
Force exerted by the floor of an elevator on the foot of a person standing on it is more than his/her weight when the elevator goes down with increasing speed.
explanation:
When the elevator goes down with increasing speeds pseudo force acts on the person in upward direction.
$\therefore$ Reaction force on person $=m(g-a)$, which is less than his weight.
For uniform speed of the elevator $a=0$, so reaction $N=m g$.
So, correct option is (2).

options.text: Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.
Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.

question: As per given figure, a weightless pulley $P$ is attached on a double inclined frictionless surfaces. The tension in the string (massless) will be (if $g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$)
explanation:
Acceleration of the blocks is given by
$a=\dfrac{m_{1} g \sin \theta_{1}-m_{2} g \sin \theta_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}$
Given,
${m_1} = 4\,kg,{m_2} = 1\,kg,{\theta _1} = 60^\circ ,{\theta _2} = 30^\circ $
$\Rightarrow a=\dfrac{\left(4 \times \sin 60^{\circ}-1 \times \sin 30^{\circ}\right)}{4+1} g$
$\Rightarrow a=\dfrac{g\left(4 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)}{5}$
$=(4 \sqrt{3}-1) m s^{-2}$
Now for, $2\,kg$ block,
$4 g \sin 60^{\circ}-T=4 \times a$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956851/preptracker/questions/q_34519_explanation_1764956851044.png $\Rightarrow T=4 \times 10 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-4(4 \sqrt{3}-1) N$
$=4(\sqrt{3}+1) N$
So, correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Pseudo force is recognised only by a non-inertial observer to explain the physical situation according to Newton's law.

Reason: Pseudo force does not exist in the action-reaction pair.
explanation: Pseudo force is an imaginary force. It comes into play only in non-inertial frames. It is not due to any of the four fundamental forces. (gravity, electromagnetic strong, weak and strong nuclear). So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

explanation: The Apparent weight of a body in an elevator moving with downward acceleration ' $a$ ' is given by $W^{\prime}=m g-m a$. Pseudo force is responsible for the lesser apparent weight. So correct option is (1).

question:
Assertion: In downward accelerated motion of lift, apparent weight of a body decreases.

Reason: When the lift moves with uniform velocity the man in the lift will feel weightlessness.
explanation:
For Assertion: Apparent weight
$W^{\prime}=m(g-a)$
where $a < g$
$\Rightarrow W^{\prime} < m g$
$\Rightarrow$ Assertion is TRUE (correct)
For Reason: When $a=g$ only with lift moving downwards, $W^{\prime}=m(g-g)=0$
It is apparently weightlessness
But, 'Reason' says lift is moving with uniform velocity.
$\Rightarrow$ Reason is FALSE
So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

explanation:

question: Three masses are connected as shown in figure on a horizontal frictionless surface and pulled by a force of $60\,N$. The tensions $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ are in the ratio
explanation:
Horizontal component of force
$ = 60\cos 30^\circ = 30\sqrt 3 \,N$
Acceleration of the system,
$a = \frac{{{\rm{ force }}}}{{(3 + 12 + 15)}} = \frac{{30\sqrt 3 }}{{30}} = \sqrt 3 \,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$
$\therefore T_{2}=$ mass pulled $\times$ acceleration
$=(3+12) \times \sqrt{3}=15 \sqrt{3} N$ and
$T_{1}=3 \times \sqrt{3}=3 \sqrt{3} N$
$\therefore \dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\dfrac{3 \sqrt{3}}{15 \sqrt{3}}=\dfrac{1}{5}$

question: Three blocks of mass $m_{1}=2.0, m_{2}=4.0$ and $m_{3}=6.0 \mathrm{~kg}$ are connected by strings on a frictionless inclined plane of $60^{\circ}$, as shown in the figure. A force $F = 120\,N$ is applied upward along the incline to the uppermost block, causing an upward movement of the blocks. The connecting cords are light. The value of tension $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ in the cords are
explanation:
The free body diagram are given below: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956848/preptracker/questions/q_34274_explanation_1764956847737.png For the motion of mass $m_{1}, m_{2}$ and $m_{3}$, we have
${T_1} = {m_1}g\cos 30^\circ = {m_1}a\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
${T_2} - {T_1} - {m_2}g\cos 30^\circ = {m_2}a\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
$F - {T_2} - {m_3}g\cos 30^\circ = {m_3}a\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
Adding Eqs., (1), (2) and (3), we get
$F = \left( {{m_1} + {m_2} + {m_3}} \right)g\cos 30^\circ $
$ = \left( {{m_1} + {m_2} + {m_3}} \right)a$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{F}{m_{1}+m_{2}+m_{3}} g \cos 30^{\circ}=a$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{120}{12}-10 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=a \Rightarrow a=10\left(1-\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$
Now, $T_{1}=m_{1}\left(a+g \cos 30^{\circ}\right)$
$ = 2\left( {10 - \frac{{10\sqrt 3 }}{2} + 10\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right) = 20\,N$
$T_{2}=a\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right)+\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) g \cos 30^{\circ}$
$ = 6 \times 10 = 60\,N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Block / Multiple Blocks Connected by a String

question: Three blocks of masses $3\,kg,6\,kg,10\,kg$ are connected to each other with light string and are then placed on a frictionless surface. The system is pulled by a forced $F=15 N$ then tension $T_{1}$ is
explanation:
Take $6\,kg$ and $10\,kg$ blocks as a single block of mass $16\,kg$
$\therefore F = ma\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} 15 = 19\,a$
$a = \frac{{15}}{{19}}\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$
${T_1} = 16\,a \Rightarrow {T_1} = 16 \times \frac{{15}}{{19}} = 12.63\,N$

question: Three equal masses $A, B$ and $C$ are pulled with a constant force $F$. They are connected to each other with strings. The ratio of the tension between $A B$ and $B C$ is
explanation:
Let $a$ be the acceleration of the system. Let $m$ be the mass of each block.
$F=\left(m_{A}+m_{B}+m_{C}\right) a$
$F = 3\,ma$
Let $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ be the tensions between $A B$ and $BC.$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951227/preptracker/questions/q_34238_explanation_1764951227477.png $T_{1}=\left(m_{B}+m_{C}\right) a$
$=2 m a=2 m\left(\dfrac{F}{3 m}\right)$
$T_{1}=\dfrac{2 F}{3}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951229/preptracker/questions/q_34238_explanation_1764951228646.png ${T_2} = \left( {{m_C}} \right)a = ma = m\left( {\frac{F}{{3m}}} \right)$
$T_{2}=\dfrac{F}{3} \Rightarrow \dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=2$
subject: Physics

question: Three blocks of mass $4\,kg,2\,kg,1\,kg$ respectively are in contact on a frictionless table as shown in the figure. If a force of $14\,N$ is applied on the $4\,kg$ block, the contact force between the $4\,kg$ and the $2\,kg$ block will be

$14-R=4 a$ or $R = 14 - 4 \times 2 = 6\,N$

So force on $A$ by $B$ is $6N$ towards left side.

question:
Assertion: A body of mass $1\,kg$ is making $1\,rps$ in a circle of radius $1\,m$. Centrifugal force acting on it is $4 \pi^{2} N$.

Reason: Centrifugal force is given by
$F=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}$.
explanation:
From relation, the centrifugal force,
$\begin{aligned}& F=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}=\dfrac{m(r \omega)^{2}}{r}=m r \omega^{2} \\& =m r(2 \pi v)^{2}=4 \pi^{2} m r v^{2} \\& =4 \pi^{2} \times 1 \times 1 \times 1^{2}=4 \pi^{2} N\end{aligned}$

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Both Assertion and reason are incorrect.

subject: Physics

question: A body of mass $5\,kg$ is suspended by a spring balance on an inclined plane as shown in figure. So, force applied as spring balance is
explanation:

$=5 \times 10 \times \dfrac{1}{2}=25 N$

question: In the figure, mass of a ball is $\dfrac{9}{5}$ times mass of the rod. Length of rod is $1\,m$. The level of ball is same as rod level. Find out time taken by the ball to reach at upper end of rod.
explanation:
Let $a_{1} \& a_{2}$ be accelerations of a ball (upward) and rod (downward) respectively. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956830/preptracker/questions/q_34166_explanation_1764956830395.png Clearly, from the diagram $2{a_1} = {a_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Now, for the ball
$2T - \frac{9}{5}mg = \frac{9}{5}m{a_1}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
and for the rod, $mg - T = m{a_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
On solving Eqs. (1),(2) & (3), we get
$a_{1}=\dfrac{g}{29} m / s^{2} \uparrow$ (upward)
$a_{2}=\dfrac{2 g}{29} m / s^{2} \downarrow$ (downward)
So, acceleration of ball w.r.t rod
$=a_{1}+a_{2}=3 \mathrm{~g} / 29$ Now, displacement of ball w.r.t. rod when it reaches the upper end of rod is $1\,m$.
Using equation of motion,
$s=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$
$s=0+\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{3 \times 10}{29} t^{2}$
$t = \sqrt {58/30} = 1.4\,s$ (approx)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Atwood Machine & Pulley Related Problems

question: In the figure, block $A$ and $B$ of masses $2 m$ and $m$ are connected with a string and system is hanged vertically with the help of a spring. Spring has negligible mass. Find out magnitude of acceleration of masses $2 m$ and $m$ just after the instant when the string of mass $m$ is cut
explanation:
When the system is in equilibrium, then the spring force is $3\,mg$. When the string is cut, then net force on block $A = 3\,mg - 2\,mg = mg$
Hence, acceleration of block $A$ at that instant $a=\dfrac{\text { Force on block A }}{\text { Mass of block A }}=\dfrac{m g}{2 m}=\dfrac{g}{2}$
When string is cut, then block $B$ falls freely with an acceleration equal to $g$.
subject: Physics

question:
Four blocks of same mass connected by strings are pulled by a force $F$ on a smooth horizontal surface as shown in figure. The tension
${T_1},{T_2}\,\& \,{T_3}$ will be
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951209/preptracker/questions/q_34158_explanation_1764951208677.png If we take the entire system $F = 4\,ma$
$\Rightarrow a=\dfrac{F}{4 m}$
$ \Rightarrow {T_3} = ma = \frac{F}{4}$
${T_2} = 2\,ma = \frac{F}{2}$
${T_1} = 3\,ma = \frac{{3F}}{4}$

question: A person is standing in an elevator. In which situation, he finds himself weightless?
explanation:
When elevator is moving downward with constant acceleration a, then net weight of the person is given by
$w^{\prime}=m(g-a)=0 \quad(\text { at } a=g)$
i.e. the person finds him weightless

options.text: When the elevator moves upward with constant acceleration
When the elevator moves downward with constant acceleration
When the elevator moves upward with uniform velocity
When the elevator moves downward with uniform velocity.

Reason: A reference in which Newton's laws of motion are applicable is non - inertial.
explanation:
A frame (vehicle) which moving with constant speed, i.e. acceleration $=0$ is an inertial frame of reference and Newton's laws of motion are applicable in it
Hence, option (4) is correct.

question: In the setup shown, a 200 $N$ block is supported in equilibrium with the help of  a string and a spring.  Extension in the spring is 4 $cm$. Force constant of the spring is closest to $[g = 10m/{s^2}]$
explanation:
Let ${T_1}$ be the tension in the spring and ${T_2}$ be the tension in the right string
in equilibrium. Acc to Lami’s theorem
$\frac{{200}}{{\sin \left( {90^\circ } \right)}} = \frac{{{T_1}}}{{\sin \left( {90 + 53} \right)}}$
$\frac{{200}}{1} = \frac{{{T_1}}}{{\cos 53}}$
${T_1} = 120\,N = Kx \Rightarrow \,K = 3000\,N/m$

question: A block of mass 1 $kg$ rests inside a cubical vessel, as shown in the figure. Which is moving with a velocity of $v = \sqrt {29} \,t\,\,m/s$ where $t$ is in sec. All the surfaces are smooth. The block is at rest w.r.t, the cube, what is the total force exerted by the cube on the block?
explanation:
Given that $V = \sqrt {29} \,t$
${\rm{a = }}\frac{{dV}}{{dt}} = \sqrt {29} $
The force exerted by the vertical wall
$R = ma = \sqrt {29} \,N$
${F_{net}} = \sqrt {{N^2} + {R^2}} $
$ = \sqrt {{{(10)}^2} + (29)} = \sqrt {129} \,N$

question: A lift moving upwards with a velocity $5\,m/s$ is stopped by uniform retardation in 5 $s$. The apparent weight of a man of mass 60 $kg$ before stopping will be (take $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$)

$N = m\left( {g - a} \right)$
$N = 60 \times 9$
$R = \frac{N}{{10}} = 54\,kg\,wt$

question: The acceleration of system of two bodies over the wedge as shown in figure is
explanation:
Let $T$ be the tension in the string
Let $a$ be acceleration of the system.
The equations of motion are
$Ma = Mg\sin 53^\circ - T\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
${\mathop{\rm and}\nolimits} \,\,Ma = T - Mg\sin 37^\circ \,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Adding (1) and (2), we get
$a = \frac{{Mg(\sin 53^\circ - \sin 37^\circ )}}{{2\,M}}$
$ = 0.98\,m{s^{ - 2}} \approx 1\,m{s^{ - 2}}$

question: In the given figure, the pulley is assumed massless and frictionless. If the friction force on the object of mass $m$ is $f$, then its acceleration in terms of the force $F$ will be equal to
explanation:

$\therefore $ Applied force $F = 2\,T$
$T = F/2\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Acceleration of block $ = \frac{{T - f}}{m}$.
$\therefore \quad a = \frac{{\frac{F}{2} - f}}{m}$

$T = \left( {\frac{2}{3}\left( {3 - y} \right) + 3} \right)\left( {15} \right)$
$T = 75 - 10\,y$

question: The elevator shown in fig. is descending with an acceleration of $6\,m/{s^2}$. The mass of block $A$ is 1$kg$. The force between $A$ and $B$ is :
explanation:
Let $N$ be the normal force between $A$ and $B$. The Newton's ${2^{nd}}$ law w.r.to lift frame on block  $A$ is
$N - mg + ma = m\left( 0 \right)$
$ \Rightarrow N = m\left( {g - a} \right)$
$ = 1\left( {10 - 6} \right) = 4N$

question: A uniform rope of mass $M$ and length $L$ is pulled up (by applying a force $F( > Mg)$ vertically. Tension in the chain at a distance $x$ from the end at which force is applied is
explanation:
Let $a$ is the acceleration of the rope
$F - Mg = Ma\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Let $T$ be the tension in the rope at a distance $x$.
$T = \frac{{\rm M}}{L}(L - x)(g + a)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From eq’s (1) & (2)
$T = F\left( {1 - \frac{x}{L}} \right)$

question: The figure shows an accelerometer: a device that  measures horizontal acceleration of cars and airplanes. The device consists of a ball that is free to roll on a parabolic track. A scale along the bottom is used to measure the ball’s horizontal position $x$. What is the acceleration of the car in  $m/{s^2}$ when the displacement of the ball in the accelerometer equals 0.20 $m$?
explanation:
The force that act on the sphere are $N$, $mg$ and pseudo force $ma$
From equilibrium
$N\cos \theta = mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$N\sin \theta = ma\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) & (2)
${\mathop{\rm Tan}\nolimits} \theta = \frac{a}{g} = \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2x\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(y = {x^2})$
$a = 2xg = 3.9$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: Two masses of 10 $kg$ and 20 $kg$ respectively, are connected by a light spring as shown in the figure. A force of 200 $N$ acts upon the 20 $kg$ mass. At the instant shown, the 10 $kg$ mass has acceleration of $12\,m/{s^2}$. The acceleration of 20 $kg$ mass is:
explanation:
The tension in the spring is
$T = 10 \times 12 = 120$
Let $a$ be the acceleration of the second block
$200 - T = 20a \Rightarrow a = 4m/{s^2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Block / Multiple Blocks Connected by a Spring

question: A trolley of mass 5 $kg$ on a horizontal smooth surface is pulled by a load of 2 $kg$ through a uniform rope $ABC$ of length 2 $m$ and mass 1 $kg$ (see fig). As the load falls from $BC = 0$ to $BC = 2\,m$, its acceleration (in $m/{s^2}$) changes from:
explanation:
Initial force $ = 2g = 20N$
Initial acceleration $\frac{{Force}}{{Mass}} = \frac{{20}}{{5 + 1}}$
$ = \frac{{20}}{6}m/{s^2}$
Final force ${\rm{ = (load}}\, + \,{\rm{mass}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{thread)}} \times g$
$ = (2 + 1) \times 10\, = \,30N$
$\therefore {\rm{Final}}\,{\rm{acceleration}}\, = \,\frac{{30}}{6}m/{s^2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Atwood Machine & Pulley Related Problems

question: A block of mass $m$ shown in figure is in equilibrium. If it is displaced further by $x$ and released find its acceleration just after it is released. Take pulleys to be light and smooth and strings light.
explanation:
Initially the block is at rest under action of force 2$T$ upward and $mg$ downwards. When the block is pulled downwards by $x$, the spring extends by 2 $x$. Hence tension $T$ increases by 2 $kx$. Thus the net unbalanced force on block of mass $m$ is 4 $kx$.
$\therefore $ Acceleration of the block $ = \frac{{4\,kx}}{m}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Atwood Machine & Pulley Related Problems

question: Two blocks $A$ and $B$ of masses 2 $m$ and $m$, respectively are connected by a massless and inextensible string. The whole system is suspended by a massless spring as shown in the figure. The magnitudes of acceleration of $A$ and $B$ immediately after the string is cut, are respectively:
explanation:
After string is cut, free body diagram of block $A$ gives
The tension in the string becomes zero.
$2m{a_A} = 3mg - 2mg$ or ${a_A} = \frac{{mg}}{{2m}} = \frac{g}{2}$
Free body diagram of block $B$ gives
$m{a_B} = mg$ or ${a_B} = g$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Block / Multiple Blocks Connected by a Spring

question: A pendulum of mass $m$ hangs from a support fixed to a trolley. The direction of the string when the trolley rolls up a plane of inclination $\alpha $ with acceleration ${a_0}$ is
explanation:
Let $T$ be the tension in the string
$T\sin \theta = m{a_0} + mg\sin \alpha $
$T\cos \theta = mg\cos \alpha $
$\tan \theta = \frac{{{a_0} + g\sin \alpha }}{{g\cos \alpha }}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: An insect of mass $m$ crawls along the hanging thread with an acceleration $a = \frac{g}{2}$. The reaction offered by ground on the block of mass 2 $m$ is:
explanation:
If the tension in the string is $T$ then for insect
$T - mg = m\frac{a}{2} \Rightarrow T = \frac{{3mg}}{2}$
$T + N = 3mg \Rightarrow N = \frac{{3mg}}{2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Atwood Machine & Pulley Related Problems

question: Two particles of mass $m$ each are tied at the ends of a light string of length 2 $a$. The whole system is kept on a frictionless horizontal surface with the string held tight so that each mass is at a distance ‘$a$’ from the centre $P$ (as shown in the figure). Now, the mid-point of the string is pulled vertically upwards with a small but constant force $F$. As a result, the particles move towards each other on the surface. The magnitude of acceleration, when the separation between them becomes 2 $x$, is
explanation:
$F = 2T\cos \theta \Rightarrow T = \frac{F}{{2\cos \theta }}$
Magnitude of acceleration of the particle
$ = \frac{{T\sin \theta }}{m} = \frac{{F\tan \theta }}{{2m}} = \frac{F}{{2m}}\,\,\frac{x}{{\sqrt {{a^2} - {x^2}} }}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Applications of Newton’s Laws

question: A light rope passes over a pulley. One section of the rope is held by a child and the other section by a man, then
explanation: Consider the man and the child to be simple blocks hung from two ends of a string passing over pulley. Being a system, the man and child both have same magnitude of acceleration but in opposite directions.

options.text: The man and the child have same vector acceleration
The man and the child have same magnitude of acceleration but in opposite direction
The man and the child have different magnitude of acceleration
The man and the child have accelerations which keep on interchanging with each other

subject: Physics
subtopic: Atwood Machine & Pulley Related Problems

question: If in a stationary lift, a man is standing with a bucket full of water, having a hole at its bottom. The rate of flow of water through this hole is ${R_0}$. If the lift starts to move up and down with same acceleration and then that rates of flow of water are ${R_u}$ and ${R_d}$ , then
explanation:
Rate of flow will be more when lift will move in upward direction with some acceleration because pseudo force acts in downward direction
${F_{upward}} = m\,(g + a)$ and ${F_{downward}} = m\,(g - a)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: Inside a railway car a plumb bob is suspended from the roof and a helium filled balloon is tied by a string to the floor of a car.  When the rail 	and car accelerates to the right, then

options.text: Both the plumb bob and balloon move to the left
Both the plumb bob and balloon move to the right
Plumb bob moves to the left and the balloon moves to the right
Plumb bob moves to the right and the balloon moves to the left

subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: A block of mass $m$ is placed on a smooth wedge of inclination  $\theta .$ The whole system is accelerated horizontally so that the block does not slip on the wedge. The force exerted by the wedge on the block ($g$ is acceleration due to gravity) will be
explanation:
When the whole system is accelerated towards left then pseudo force ($ma$) acts on a block towards right.
For the condition of equilibrium
$mg\sin \theta = ma\cos \theta \Rightarrow a = \frac{{g\sin \theta }}{{\cos \theta }}$
$\therefore $ Force exerted by the wedge on the block
$R = mg\cos \theta + ma\sin \theta $
$R = mg\cos \theta + m\left( {\frac{{g\sin \theta }}{{\cos \theta }}} \right)\sin \theta $
$ = \frac{{mg({{\cos }^2}\theta + {{\sin }^2}\theta )}}{{\cos \theta }}$
$R = \frac{{mg}}{{\cos \theta }}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: A simple pendulum is vibrating with an angular amplitude of $\frac{\pi }{2}$. The value of $\alpha $ for which the resultant acceleration is along the horizontal is
explanation:
From conservation of energy
$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = mgl\cos \alpha $
$ \Rightarrow {v^2} = 2gl\cos \alpha $
As the acceleration is along horizontal direction. The vertical components should get cancelled
${a_r}\cos \alpha = {a_t}\sin \alpha $
$\frac{{{v^2}}}{l}\cos \alpha = g{\sin ^2}\alpha $
$\frac{{2gl{{\cos }^2}\alpha }}{l} = g{\sin ^2}\alpha $
${\tan ^2}\alpha = 2$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Applications of Newton’s Laws

question: A coin is placed on a horizontal platform which undergoes vertical simple harmonic motion of angular frequency   $\omega $. The amplitude of oscillation is gradually increased. The coin will leave contact with the platform for the first time
explanation:
As the amplitude is increased, the maximum acceleration of the platform increases.
If we draw the FBD for coin at one of the extreme position as shown
From Newton’s law, $mg - N = m{\omega ^2}A$
For loosing contact with the platform, $N = 0$
So, $A = g/{\omega ^2}$

options.text: At the mean position of the platform
At the highest position of the platform

subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Applications of Newton’s Laws

question: Find the acceleration of 3 $kg$ mass when acceleration of 2 $kg$ mass is  $2m{s^{ - 2}}$ as shown in figure.
explanation:
Let $kx$ be the tension in the spring
$10 - kx = 2 \times 2$
$kx = 6N$
$\therefore {\mathop{\rm Acceleration}\nolimits} \,\,of\,\,3\,{\rm{kg}} = \frac{6}{3} = 2\,\,{\rm{m/}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Block / Multiple Blocks Connected by a Spring

question: Two blocks of mass 2 $kg$ are connected by a massless ideal spring of spring constant $K = 10\,{\rm{N/m}}{\rm{.}}$ The upper block is suspended from roof by a light string $A$. The system shown is in equilibrium. The string $A$ is now cut, the acceleration of upper block just after the string $A$ is cut will be $(g = 10\,{\rm{m/}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}})$
explanation:
Before the string $A$ is cut:
Let $x$ be elongation in the spring.
As system is in equilibrium.
Then for lower block,
$kx = mg = 20N$
Just after the string $A$ is cut:
For upper block,
$ma = kx + mg$
$ \Rightarrow \,\quad 2a = 20 + 20$
$ \Rightarrow \,\quad a = 20\,m/{s^2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Block / Multiple Blocks Connected by a Spring

question: A block of mass 3 $kg$ is initially in equilibrium and is hanging by two identical springs $A$ and $B$ as shown in figure. If spring $A$ is cut from lower point at $t = 0$ then, find acceleration of block in $m{s^{ - 2}}$ at $t = 0$. (ignore rotational effect.)
explanation:
Before cutting the spring
$2kx = 3g \Rightarrow kx = 15$
After cutting
$a = \frac{{3g - kx}}{3} = \frac{{15}}{3} = 5m/{s^2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Block / Multiple Blocks Connected by a Spring

question: Sixteen beads in a string are placed on a smooth inclined plane of inclination sin-1(1/3) such that some of them lie along the incline whereas the rest hang over the top of the plane. If acceleration at first bead is $g$/2, the arrangement of beads is that
explanation:
If $n$ balls each of mass $m$ are hanging vertically then, $nmg - T = nma = \frac{{nmg}}{2}$
or $T = \frac{{nmg}}{2}$
also $T - (16 - n)mg\sin \theta $
$ = (16 - n)mg/2$
on $\frac{{nmg}}{2} - (16 - n)mg\frac{1}{3} = (16 - n)\frac{{mg}}{2}$ (testing (1))
or $n\frac{4}{3} = \frac{{80}}{6}$
or $n = \frac{{80}}{6} \times \frac{3}{4} = 10$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Miscellaneous Applications of Newton’s Laws

question: Two blocks are connected by a spring. The combination is suspended, at rest, from a string attached to the ceiling, as shown in the figure. The string breaks suddenly. Immediately after the string breaks, what is the initial downward acceleration of the upper block of mass 2 $m$?
subject: Physics
subtopic: Block / Multiple Blocks Connected by a Spring

question: If the system shown in figure is released from 	rest then acceleration of 2 $kg$ block is (take 	 $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$ and the pulleys are very light)
explanation:
Let $T$ be the tension in the string connected to block of mass 2 $kg$. The tensions in other parts are shown in the figure.
$T - 20 = 2\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$60 - 2T = 6\left( {\frac{a}{2}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From the above two eq’s we get
$a = \frac{{20}}{7}m/{s^2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Atwood Machine & Pulley Related Problems

question: A bead of mass $m$ is attached to one end spring of natural length $R$ and spring constant $K = \frac{{(1 + \sqrt 3 )mg}}{R}$. The other end of the spring is fixed at a point $A$ on a smooth vertical ring of radius $R$. The normal reaction at $B$ just after it is released to move is:
explanation:
The extension in the spring is $x$
$x = AB - R = 2R\cos 30^\circ  - R$
$x = R(\sqrt 3  - 1)$
Forces -acting on the bead are shown in the figure
  https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950049/preptracker/questions/q_33366_explanation_1764950048884.png
$N = mg\cos 30^\circ  + F\cos 30^\circ $
$ = mg\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} + KR(\sqrt 3  - 1)\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} = \frac{{3\sqrt 3 mg}}{2}$
$\left( {K = \frac{{(1 + \sqrt 3 )mg}}{R}} \right)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Block / Multiple Blocks Connected by a Spring

question: The magnitude of relative acceleration of the two blocks is (Pulleys and threads are massless)($g = 10\,m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
Let $T$ be the tension in the string.
$T = 30\,N$
Let ${a_1}$ and ${a_2}$ be the accelerations of the blocks w.r.to ground.
For 4$kg$ block
$4g - 30 = 4{a_1} \Rightarrow {a_1} = 2.5m/{s^2}$
For 2$kg$ block
$30 - 2g = 2{a_2} \Rightarrow {a_2} = 5m/{s^2}$
$\left| {{a_{21}}} \right| = \left| {{a_2}} \right| + \left| {{a_1}} \right|$
${a_{21}} = 7.5\,m/s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Atwood Machine & Pulley Related Problems

question: A massless string of length $l$ passes over a frictionless pulley with horizontal axis. Two monkeys hang from the ends of the string at the distance $l$/2 from the pulley, a monkey start climbing up with a speed $v$ relative to the string and the second with speed of 2 $v$. Both monkeys have got same masses. The time taken by the first and second monkeys in reaching the pulleys are respectively.
explanation:
Let $u$ be the velocity of the rope. ${v_1}\,\& \,\,{v_2}$ are the velocities of the monkeys w.r.to ground.
${v_1} = u + v$ $\& \,{v_2} = 2v - u$
From conservation of momentum
$m\left( {v + u} \right) - m\left( {2v - u} \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \,u = v/2,{v_1} = \frac{{3v}}{2}\& {v_2} = \frac{{3v}}{2}$
Time taken by the monkeys to reach the pulley is $t = \frac{{\ell /2}}{{3v/2}} = \left( {\frac{\ell }{{3v}}} \right)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Atwood Machine & Pulley Related Problems

question: The apparent weight of a person inside a lift is ${W_1}$ when lift moves up with a certain acceleration and is ${W_2}$ when lift moves down with same acceleration. The weight of the person when lift moves up with constant speed  is:
explanation:
Let $a$ is the acceleration of the lift. When the lift is going up
$N - mg = ma\quad \Rightarrow N = ma + mg = {W_1}$
When the lift is going down
$mg - N = ma\quad \Rightarrow N = mg - ma = {W_2}$
From the above two eq’s we get
$a = \frac{{{W_1} - {W_2}}}{{2m}} \Rightarrow mg = \frac{{{W_1} + {W_2}}}{2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: A lift moving upwards with a velocity 10 $m$/$s$ is stopped by uniform retardation in 5 $s$. The apparent weight of a man of mass 60 $kg$ before stopping will be (take $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
The deceleration $a = \frac{{10}}{5} = 2m/{s^2}$
The normal force or apparent weight is $N = m\left( {g - a} \right)$
$N = 60 \times 8$
$R = \frac{N}{{10}} = 48kg\,wt$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: A lift is moving down with acceleration $a$. A man in the lift drops a ball inside the lift. The acceleration of the ball as observed by the man in the lift and a man standing stationary on the ground are respectively
explanation:
For the man standing in the left , the acceleration of the ball
${\overrightarrow a _{b\ell }} = {\overrightarrow a _b} - {\overrightarrow a _\ell } \Rightarrow {a_{b\ell }} = g - a$
Where $'{a_\ell }'$ is the acceleration of the lift
(because the acceleration of the lift is $'a'$)
For the man standing on the ground, the acceleration of the ball
${\overrightarrow a _{bg}} = {\overrightarrow a _b} - {\overrightarrow a _g} \Rightarrow {a_{bg}} = g - 0 = g$
${a_g} = 0$ ( acceleration of the ground)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question:
A bob is hanging inside a car through a massless string as shown in figure. The car is moving with constant acceleration $a$, horizontally
The bob makes an angle $\theta $ with the vertical at equilibrium. Then which of the following statement is correct?
explanation:
$\tan \theta = \frac{a}{g}$
So, $\theta $ is directly proportional to $a$. If $a$ decreases then $\theta $ decreases.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: A pseudo force

options.text: $ - m\overrightarrow a $, where $a = $ acceleration of the object of mass $m$.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question:
A block is placed on a rough horizontal plane attached with an elastic spring as shown in the figure.
Intially the spring is unstretched. If the plane is gradually lifted from \[\theta = 0^\circ \] to \[\theta = 90^\circ ,\] then the graph showing extension in the spring (\[x\]) versus angle \[\theta \] is.
explanation:
Extension in the spring is zero until
$mg\sin \theta = \mu mg\cos \theta $
Afterwards gradually $x$ increases.
When $\theta > {\tan ^{ - 1}}(\mu )$
$ \Rightarrow x = \frac{{mg\sin \theta - \mu mg\cos \theta }}{K}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Block / Multiple Blocks Connected by a Spring

question: Two masses 8 $kg$ 12 $kg$ are connected at the two ends of a light inextensible string that goes over a frictionless pulley. The acceleration of the masses and the tension in the string when the masses are released, are respectively
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950031/preptracker/questions/q_33226_explanation_1764950031141.png Given that
${m_1} = 8\,kg,\,\,{m_2} = 12\,kg$
Let $T$ be the tension in the string and masses moves with an acceleration $a$ when masses are released.
$\therefore \quad a = \frac{{({m_2} - {m_1})}}{{({m_2} + {m_2})}}g$
$a = \frac{{12 - 8}}{{12 + 8}} \times 10 = 2\,\,m/{s^2}$
The tension in the string is
$T = {m_1}g + {m_1}a$
$ = {m_1}(g + a) = 8\,(10 + 2) = 90N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Block / Multiple Blocks Connected by a String

question:
Assertion: A reference frame attached to earth is an inertial frame of reference.

Reason: The reference frame which has zero acceleration is called a non inertial frame of reference.
explanation: An inertial frame of reference is one which has zero acceleration. The earth rotates about its own axis. That is why earth cannot be taken as inertial frame of reference.

options.text: Both assertion and reason are correct and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
Both assertion and reason are correct but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
Assertion is correct but reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: Two blocks  $A$ and $B$ of masses 3 $m$ and $m$ respectively are connected by a massless and inextensible string. The whole system is suspended by a massless spring as shown in 	figure. The magnitudes of acceleration of $A$ and $B$ immediately after the string is cut, are respectively
explanation:
Before cutting the string https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956825/preptracker/questions/q_33149_explanation_1764956825124.png $\therefore \,T = mg$
After cutting the string https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956826/preptracker/questions/q_33149_explanation_1764956825902.png ${a_A} = \frac{{4mg - 3mg}}{{3m}} = \frac{g}{3}$
${a_B} = \frac{{mg}}{m} = g$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Block / Multiple Blocks Connected by a Spring

question: A particle moving with velocity  ${\vec V}$ is acted by three forces shown by the vector triangle $PQR$. The velocity of the particle will :
explanation:
As forces are forming closed loop in same order
So, ${\overrightarrow F _{net}} = 0 \Rightarrow \vec a = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\overrightarrow v = {\rm{constant}}$

options.text: Change according to the smallest force $\overrightarrow {QR} $

question: If the elevator in the shown figure is moving upwards with  constant acceleration $1\,m/{s^2}$, the tension in the string connected to block $A$ of mass 6 $kg$ would be ($g = 10\,m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
$T - {m_A}g = {m_A}a$
$\therefore \,\,T = {m_A}\left( {g + a} \right)$
$ = 6(10 + 1) = 66N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: A simple pendulum is suspended from the ceiling of a lift. When the lift is at rest its time period is $T$. With what  acceleration should the lift be accelerated upwards in order to reduce its period to $T/2$? ($g$ is acceleration due to gravity).
explanation:
Let $a$ be the acceleration of the lift in upward direction. The effective acceleration is ${a_r} = a + g$
$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{g}} $
$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{{{a_r}}}} $
$T' = \frac{T}{2} \Rightarrow {a_r} = 4g$
The lift should be accelerated by 3 $g$ upwards .
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: A person of mass 60 $kg$ is inside a lift of mass  940 $kg$ and presses the button on control panel. The lift starts moving upwards with an acceleration  $1.0\,m/{s^2}.$ If  $g = \,10\,m/{s^2},$ the tension in the supporting cable is
explanation:
Total mass $(m) = $ Mass of lift $+$ Mass of person
$ = 940 + 60 = 1000\,kg$
$T - mg = ma$
Hence, $T - 1000 \times 10 = 1000 \times 1$
$T = 11000\,N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: A body of mass 50 $kg$ is suspended using a spring balance inside a lift at rest. If the lift starts falling freely, the reading of the spring balance is
explanation:
Reading of the spring balance
$ = m(g - a) = 50(g - g) = 0$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: A man weighing 60 $kg$ is in a lift moving down with an acceleration of $1.8\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. The force exerted by the floor on him is
explanation:
Given that
$m = 60\,kg$
$a = 1.8\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
As the lift moving down with acceleration $a$, the force exerted by the floor on the man is
$N = mg - ma = m(g - a)$
$ = (60\,kg)\,(9.8\,m{s^{ - 2}} - 1.8\,m{s^{ - 2}})$
$ = (60\,kg)\,(8.0\,m{s^{ - 2}}) = 480\,N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: In a lift moving up with an acceleration of  $5\,m{s^{ - 2}}$, a ball is dropped from a height of $1.25\,m$. The time taken by the ball to reach the floor of the lift is ______ (nearly) ($g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$)
explanation:
Time taken by the ball to reach the floor is
$t = \sqrt {\frac{{2h}}{{a + g}}} $
Here, $h = 1.25\,m,g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}},a = 5\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
$\therefore \,t = \sqrt {\frac{{2 \times 1.25\,m}}{{(5 + 10)m{s^{ - 2}}}}} = 0.4\,s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: The pendulum hanging from the ceiling of a railway carriage makes an angle $30^\circ $ with the vertical when it is accelerating. The acceleration of the carriage is
explanation: $T\cos \theta  = mg$ and $T\sin \theta  = ma$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955613/preptracker/questions/q_33014_explanation_1764955612663.png From these two equations we get, $a = g\tan \theta  = g\tan 30^\circ  = \frac{g}{{\sqrt 3 }}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question: A balloon with mass $m$ is descending down with an acceleration $a$ (where $a < g$). How much mass should be removed from it so that it starts moving up with an acceleration $a$
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949983/preptracker/questions/q_33006_explanation_1764949982215.png $mg - B = ma\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$B - (m - {m_0})g = (m - {m_0})a\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Equation (1) + equation (2)
$ \Rightarrow \,\,mg - mg + {m_0}g = ma + ma - {m_0}a$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,{m_0} = \frac{{2ma}}{{g + a}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-inertial Frame & Pseudo Force

question:
Read the ${\rm{Statement - A}}$ and ${\rm{Statement - B}}$ carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Aristotle believed that continuous force is required to maintain motion.
A force is necessary in practice only to counteract friction, not to maintain uniform motion.
explanation: Statement A is true as Aristotle's incorrect view was that a continuous force is necessary for continuous motion. Statement B is also true; in the absence of friction (or any other opposing forces), no force is necessary to maintain uniform motion. The force is only needed to counteract friction when it is present. Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Aristotle’s Fallacy

question:
Read the ${\rm{Statement - A}}$ and ${\rm{Statement - B}}$ carefully to mark the correct option given below:
A stone released from a height accelerates downward due to an external force, which is the gravitational pull of the Earth.
A moving body on a frictionless surface, like a skater on ice, requires an external force to continue moving with constant speed.
explanation: Statement A is true as it correctly describes the reason for the acceleration of a stone falling under gravity, which is an external force. Statement B is false because, according to Newton's first law of motion, a body in uniform motion on a frictionless surface does not require an external force to keep moving; it will continue to move with constant speed in a straight line unless acted upon by an external force. Correct option is (2).

question: A spherical bob of mass $250\,g$ is attached to the end of a string having length $50\,cm$. The bob is rotated on a horizontal circular path about a vertical axis. The maximum tension that the string can bear is $72\,N$. The maximum possible value of angular velocity of bob (in $rad/s$) is
explanation:
For rotating bob, tension provides necessary centripetal force. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952267/preptracker/questions/q_107685_explanation_1764952267186.png $ \Rightarrow \quad T = mr{\omega ^2}$
Here, $T = 72\,N,m = 250\,g = 0.25\,kg$
So, $72 = 0.25 \times 0.5 \times {\omega ^2}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,mr{\omega ^2} = 69.5 \Rightarrow {\omega ^2} = \frac{{72}}{{0.25 \times 0.5}} = 576$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\omega = \sqrt {576} \approx 24\,\,rad/s$
So, the correct option is (2).

question: A car is moving on circular track banked at an angle of $45^\circ $. If the maximum permissible speed of the car to avoid slipping is twice the optimum speed of the car to avoid the wear and tear of the tyres, then the coefficient of static friction between the wheels of the car and the road is
explanation:
Maximum speed $v_{\text {max }}=\sqrt{\dfrac{r g(\mu+\tan \theta)}{1-\mu \tan \theta}}$
and optimum speed $v_{0}=\sqrt{r g \tan \theta}$
Given $\theta=45^{\circ}$ and $v_{\text {max }}=2 v_{0}$
$\sqrt{\dfrac{r g(\mu+\tan \theta)}{1-\mu \tan \theta}}=2 \sqrt{r g \tan \theta}$
Squaring both sides $\dfrac{(\mu+\tan \theta)}{(1-\mu \tan \theta)}=4 \tan \theta$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\mu+\tan 45^{\circ}}{1-\mu \tan 45^{\circ}}=4 \tan 45^{\circ} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\mu+1}{1-\mu}=4$
$\Rightarrow \mu+1=4-4 \mu \Rightarrow 5 \mu=3 \therefore \mu=\dfrac{3}{5}=0.6$

question: An aircraft executes a horizontal loop of radius $9\,km$ at a constant speed of $540\,km{h^{ - 1}}$. The wings of the aircraft are banked at an angle of (Acceleration due to gravity $ = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$)
explanation:
For circular motion of aircraft, $r = 9\,km,v = 540\,km\,{h^{ - 1}}$
$ = 540 \times \frac{5}{{18}} = 150\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952266/preptracker/questions/q_107681_explanation_1764952265697.png If $F_{L}$ be the lift force on the air craft wings, then $F_{L} \sin \theta=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}$ and $F_{L} \cos \theta=m g$
$\therefore$ Banked angle, $\tan \theta=\dfrac{v^{2}}{r g}=\dfrac{150 \times 150}{9 \times 10^{3} \times 10}=\dfrac{1}{4}$
$\therefore \theta=\cot ^{-1}(4)$
