question: If the radii of circular path of two particles of same mass are in the ratio of $1: 2$, then to have a constant centripetal force, the ratio of their speeds should be
explanation:
Given,
Ratio of radius of two circular paths $=1: 2$
Ratio of masses $=1: 1$
Centripetal force is constant.Centripetal force is given by $F_{c}=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}$
$F_{c_{1}}=\dfrac{m_{1} v_{1}^{2}}{r_{1}} \quad\left[\right.$ For $1^{\text {st }}$ particle $]$
$F_{c_{2}}=\dfrac{m_{2} v_{2}^{2}}{r_{2}} \quad\left[\right.$ For $2^{\text {nd }}$ particle $]$
$F_{c_{1}}=F_{c_{2}} \quad$ [given]$\dfrac{m_{1} v_{1}^{2}}{r_{1}}=\dfrac{m_{2} v_{2}^{2}}{r_{2}}$
$\dfrac{v_{1}^{2}}{v_{2}^{2}}=\dfrac{m_{2} r_{1}}{m_{1} r_{2}} \quad\left[m_{1}=m_{2}, \dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{2}(\right.$ given $\left.)\right]$
$\dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
Hence, the ratio of speed is $1: \sqrt{2}$.
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A cyclist is riding with a speed of $36 {~km} / {h}$. As he approaches a circular turn on the road of radius $50 m$, he applies brakes and reduces his speed at the constant rate of $0.5 {~m} / {s}$ every second. The magnitude and direction (with the radial line) of the net acceleration of the cyclist on the circular turn respectively, are
explanation:
Cyclist has a centripetal acceleration $a_{c}$ and a tangential acceleration $a_{t}$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957825/preptracker/questions/q_107594_explanation_1764957825120.png So, net acceleration of cyclist is
$a=\sqrt{a_{c}^{2}+a_{t}^{2}}$
Here, $a_{c}=\dfrac{v^{2}}{r}$ and $a_{t}=\dfrac{d v}{d t}$
From given data we have,
$a_{c}=\left(36 \times \dfrac{5}{18}\right)^{2} / 50=2 {~ms}^{-2}$
and $a_{t}=\dfrac{0.5 m s^{-2}}{1}=\dfrac{1}{2} m s^{-2}$
so, $a=\sqrt{2^{2}+\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{17}}{2} m s^{-2}$
Also, angle $\theta$ is such that,
$\tan \theta=\dfrac{a_{t}}{a_{c}}=\dfrac{1 / 2}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1}(1 / 4)$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: What will be the minimum speed of the roller-coaster, so that the passenger at the top when becomes upside down, do not fall out? Consider the acceleration due to gravity as $10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ and the radius of curvature of the roller-coaster is $10\,m$.
explanation:
Given, $R=10 {~m}$
$g=10 {~ms}^{-2}$
Let the minimum speed of roller coaster is $v$.
At the top of the roller coaster,
$N+m g=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{R}[N=$ normal reaction $]$
But $N=0$ for maximum speed.
$\therefore 0+m g=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{R}$
$\Rightarrow v=\sqrt{g R}$.
$\therefore \quad v=\sqrt{10 \times 10}=\sqrt{100}=10 {~ms}^{-1}$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A car moves on a horizontal circular road of radius $16 m$ with increasing speed at a constant rate of $3 {~m} / {s}^{2}$. If the coefficient of friction between the road and the tyre is 0.5, then the minimum speed at which the car will skid is (Assume, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$)
explanation:
Given, radius of road, $R=16 m$,
Tangential acceleration, $a_{T}=3 {~ms}^{-2}$,
Radial acceleration $=a_{N}$,
Coefficient of friction, $\mu=0.5$
and acceleration due to gravity, $g=10 {~ms}^{-2}$
Let minimum speed for skid is $v$.
As we know that,$f=\mu N=m \sqrt{a_{N}^{2}+a_{T}^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \mu m g=m \sqrt{a_{N}^{2}+a_{T}^{2}}$
where, $f, N$ and $m$ are limiting friction force, normal reaction and mass of body, respectively.
$\Rightarrow \mu g=\sqrt{a_{N}^{2}+a_{T}^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow 0.5 \times 10=\sqrt{a_{N}^{2}+3^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow 25=a_{N}^{2}+9$
$\Rightarrow a_{N}^{2}=25-9=16 \Rightarrow a_{N}=4 m s^{-2}$
But $a_{N}=\dfrac{v^{2}}{R}$
$\Rightarrow v^{2}=a_{N} R \Rightarrow v=\sqrt{4 \times 16}$
$\Rightarrow v=\sqrt{64}=8 m s^{-1}$
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A car is moving with velocity $v$ at the top of a semi-circular hill of radius $40 m$ such that the normal force on it is zero. Find the velocity(v) of the car. [Use, $g=10 {~ms}^{-2}$ ]
explanation:
Given that, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
Velocity of car at top of hill $=v$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952242/preptracker/questions/q_107584_explanation_1764952242117.png Radius of semi-circular hill, $R=40 {~cm}$
Let mass of car $=m$
Now, the centripetal force is equal to the net force acting on car at top.
$\therefore F_{c}=m g-N \quad[$ Given, ${N}=0]$
$\dfrac{m v^{2}}{R}=m g \Rightarrow v=\sqrt{g R}=\sqrt{10 \times 40}=20 {~m} / {s}$
So, the correct option is (2)

subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A circular freeway entrance and exit are commonly banked to control a moving car at $14 {~m} / {s}$. To design similar ramp for $28 {~m} / {s}$ one should
explanation:
Given, $v_{1}=14 {~m} / {s}$ and $v_{2}=28 {~m} / {s}$
As we know that, $\tan \theta=\dfrac{v^{2}}{r g}$
$\Rightarrow r=\dfrac{v^{2}}{g \tan \theta}$
For a given $\theta, r \propto v^{2}$
$\therefore \dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{v_{2}}{v_{1}}\right)^{2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{28}{14}\right)^{2}=r_{2}=4 r_{1}$
Hence, radius should be increased by factor 4.
So, the correct option is (2)

question: A cyclist leans with the horizontal at angle $30^{\circ}$, while negotiating round a circular road of radius $20 \sqrt{3} {~m}$. The speed of the cycle should be
explanation:
According to the question, the situation is as shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952229/preptracker/questions/q_107575_explanation_1764952228914.png Here $N \cos 30^{\circ}=$ centripetal force
Balancing the forces,
$N\sin \,30^\circ = mg{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( 1 \right)$
and $N\cos \,30^\circ = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{R}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( 2 \right)$
Dividing Eq. (1) by Eq. (2), we get
$\tan 30^{\circ}=\dfrac{g R}{v^{2}} \Rightarrow v^{2}=\dfrac{g R}{\tan 30^{\circ}}$
$\Rightarrow v^{2}=10 \times 20 \sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{3} \quad\left[\because \tan 30^{\circ}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right]$
$\Rightarrow v=\sqrt{600}=10 \sqrt{6} {~m} / {s}$
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: Assume proton is rotating along a circular path of radius 1 m under a centrifugal force of $4 \times 10^{-12} N$. If the mass of proton is $1.6 \times 10^{-27} {~kg}$, then its angular velocity of rotation is
explanation:
Given, $r=1 m$
$F_{\text {centrifugal }}=4 \times 10^{-12} {~N}, m=1.6 \times 10^{-27} {~kg}$
$\omega=$ ?
$\because \quad F_{\text {centrifugal }}=m \omega^{2} r \Rightarrow \omega^{2}=\dfrac{F_{\text {centrifugal }}}{m r}$
$\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{F_{\text {centrifugal }}}{m r}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 10^{-12}}{1.6 \times 10^{-27} \times 1}}$
$=\sqrt{\dfrac{10^{-12}}{0.4 \times 10^{-27}}}=\sqrt{25 \times 10^{15}}$
$=\sqrt{25 \times 10^{14}}=5 \times 10^{7} {rad} / {s}$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A coin is placed on a disc. The coefficient of friction between the coin and the disc is $\mu$. If the distance of the coin from the center of the disc is $r,$ the maximum angular velocity which can be given to the disc, so that the coin does not slip away is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955893/preptracker/questions/q_35442_explanation_1764955892690.png
For no slipping
$\begin{aligned}& f=m r \omega^{2} \\& \Rightarrow \mu n=m r \omega^{2} \\& \Rightarrow \mu m g=m r \omega^{2}(\because N=m g) \\& \therefore \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu g}{r}}\end{aligned}$

question: If the radius of curvature of the path of two particles of same mass are in the ratio $3: 4$, then in order to have constant centripetal force, their velocities  will be in the ratio of
explanation:
As per question
$F = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} = $ Constant
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{v^2}}}{r} = $ Constant $ \Rightarrow {v^2}\alpha \,r$
$ \Rightarrow v\,\alpha \sqrt r $
$\therefore \frac{{{v_1}}}{{{v_2}}} = \frac{{\sqrt {{r_1}} }}{{\sqrt {{r_2}} }} = \sqrt {\frac{3}{4}} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}$

question: A car of mass  $800\,kg$  is taking turn on a banked road of radius $300\,m$  and angle of banking $30^{\circ}$. If coefficient of static friction is 0.2 then the maximum speed with which car can negotiate the turn safely  $\left( {g = 10\,m/{s^2},\sqrt 3  = 1.73} \right)$
explanation:
$m = 500\,kg,$ $r = 300\,m$
$\theta = 30^\circ ,{\mu _s} = 0.2,g = 10\,m/{s^2}$
Maximum safe speed on the banking rough curved road is
$v_{\max }=\sqrt{r g\left(\dfrac{\tan \theta+\mu_{s}}{1-\mu_{s} \tan \theta}\right)}$
$\Rightarrow v_{\max }=\sqrt{300 \times 10\left(\dfrac{\tan 30^{\circ}+0.2}{1-0.2 \tan 30^{\circ}}\right)}$
$ \Rightarrow {v_{\max }} = \sqrt {3000\left( {\frac{{\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }} + 0.2}}{{1 - 0.2 \times \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}}}} \right)} = 51.4\,m/s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: $A \operatorname{rod} A B$ of length 2 $m$ is hinged at point $A$ and its other end $B$ is attached to a platform on which a block of mass $m$ is kept. Rod rotates about point $A$ maintaining angle $\theta=30^{\circ}$ with the vertical in such a way that platform remains horizontal and revolves on the horizontal circular path. If the coefficient of static friction between the block and platform is $\mu=0.1$, then find the maximum angular velocity   of rod so that the block does not slip on the platform. $\left(g=10 {~m} {~s}^{-2}\right)$
explanation:
$N = mg,\mu N = mr\,\,{\omega ^2}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955874/preptracker/questions/q_35331_explanation_1764955873989.png $\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\mu mg = m2\sin \theta {\omega ^2}$
$ \Rightarrow \omega = \sqrt {\frac{{\mu g}}{{2\sin \theta }}} = \sqrt {\frac{{0.1 \times 10}}{{2 \times \sin 30}}} = 1\,\,rad{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: A small block of mass $1\,kg$ is held by a light string rests on a smooth inclined plane which can turn about ${Z Z^{\prime}}$-axis with an angular velocity $2\,rad/s$ as shown in the figure. The block is at distance ${\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} {~m}}$ from the point ${O}$. The tension in the string is ${(32)^{x} {~N}}$. Find the value of ${x}$ is
explanation:
${N \cos 45^{\circ}+T \cos 45^{\circ}=m g}$
(in vertical direction)
Or ${\dfrac{N}{\sqrt{2}}+\dfrac{T}{\sqrt{2}}=10}$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} N + T = 10\sqrt 2 \,N\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955847/preptracker/questions/q_35126_explanation_1764955846928.png Also, in horizontal direction
$N\sin 45^\circ - T\sin 45^\circ $
$ = mr{\omega ^2} = m \times \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\sin 45^\circ \times {2^2}$
Or ${\dfrac{N}{\sqrt{2}}-\dfrac{T}{\sqrt{2}}=2}$
Or $N - T = 2\sqrt 2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$T = 4\sqrt 2 N = \sqrt {2 \times 16} = {(32)^{1/2}}$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} x = 2$
subject: Physics

question: A car starts from rest and starts moving on a circular path of radius 100 $m$ such that its speed increases at the rate of ${5 {~m} {~s}^{-2}}$. What is the radial acceleration of the car at the instant it makes one complete round trip (in ${{m} {s}^{-2}}$ )?
explanation:
The speed of the car after one complete round trip will be
${v^2} = {u^2} + 2as.$
${v^2} = 2 \times 5\,(2\pi r)\,\,\,[u = 0\,\,\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\,s = 2\pi r]$
${v^2} = 20\pi r$
Radial acceleration of the particle will be$a_{r}=\dfrac{v^{2}}{r}=\dfrac{20 \pi r}{r}=20 \pi {m} / {s}^{2}$.
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A particle is projected horizontally with a speed ${15 {~m} {~s}^{-1}}$ at a height 200 $m$ above the ground at time ${t=0}$. What is the tangential acceleration of the particle at time ${t=2 {sec}}$ ? Assume ${g=10 {~m} {~s}^{2}}$.
explanation:
Given: ${u_{x}=15 {~m} / {s}, h=200 {~m}}$$u_{y}=0, a_{x}=0, a_{y}=g$
Velocity of the particle in ${x}$ and ${y}$ direction at ${t=2 {~s}}$
$\begin{aligned}& v_{y}=u_{y}+a_{y} t=g t=10 \times 2=20 {~m} / {s} \\& v_{x}=15 {~m} / {s}\end{aligned}$
The angle between two velocities ${v_{x}}$ and ${v_{y}}$ at ${t=2 {~s}}$ is shown in the figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955823/preptracker/questions/q_34907_explanation_1764955823094.png Now, ${\tan \theta=\dfrac{v_{y}}{v_{x}}=\dfrac{20}{15}=\dfrac{4}{3}}$
Also the component of the acceleration at $t = 2$ is shown in the figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955824/preptracker/questions/q_34907_explanation_1764955824185.png The tangential acceleration at $t = 2\,s$ is ${g_t} = g\sin \theta $
$ = 10 \times \frac{4}{5} = 8\,m/{s^2}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \left[ {\because \tan \theta = \frac{4}{3}} \right]$.
So correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A coin placed on a rotating turn table just slips, if it is placed at a distance of $8\,cm$ from the centre. If angular speed of the turn table is doubled, then it will just slip at a distance of
explanation:
A body placed on a non-inertial frame of
reference which is rotating about its axis,
experiences a centrifugal force.
It is given by
$F=m r \omega^{2}$
where, $r$ is radius of circle, $m$ is mass and $\omega$ is
angular speed.
$\therefore \dfrac{F_{1}}{F_{2}}=\dfrac{r_{1} \omega_{1}^{2}}{r_{2} \omega_{2}^{2}}$
Since, $F_{1}=F_{2}$
$\Rightarrow r_{1} \omega_{1}^{2}=r_{2} \omega_{2}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad r_{2}=\dfrac{r_{1} \omega_{1}^{2}}{\omega_{2}^{2}}$
Given, $r_{1}=8 \mathrm{~cm}, \omega_{1}=\omega$ and $\omega_{2}=2 \omega$
$\therefore \quad {r_2} = \frac{{8 \times {\omega ^2}}}{{{{(2\omega )}^2}}} = \frac{8}{4} = 2\,cm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A ball of mass $0.25\,kg$ attached to the ends of a string of length $1.96\,m$ is rotating in a horizontal circle. The string will break, if tension is more than $25 N$. What is the maximum velocity with which the ball can be rotated?
explanation:
The centripetal force,
$F=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}$
$\Rightarrow v^{2}=\underline{F r}$
$\Rightarrow v^{2}=\dfrac{25 \times 1.96}{0.25}=196$
$\therefore v = \sqrt {196} = 14\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A car is moving in a circular horizontal track of radius $10.0\,m$ with a constant speed of  $10.0\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$ A plumb bob is suspended from the roof of the car by a light rigid rod of length  $10.0\,m.$  The angle made by the rod with the track is (take, $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ )
explanation:
If angle of banking is $\theta$, then
$\tan \theta=\dfrac{m v^{2} / r}{m g} \Rightarrow \tan \theta=\dfrac{v^{2}}{r g}$
Given, $v = 10,r = 10\,m$
and $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
So, $\tan \theta=\dfrac{(10)^{2}}{10 \times 10}=1$
$\therefore \theta=45^{\circ}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A circular race track of radius $300\,\,m$ is banked at an angle of $15^{\circ}$. If the coefficient of friction between the wheels of the race car and the road is 0.2 . Find optimum speed of the race car to avoid wear and tear on its tyres and maximum permissible speed to avoid slipping.
explanation:
Here, $\mu_{s}=0.2, R=300 m, \theta=15^{\circ}$
$\nu_{o}($ optimal velocity $)=\sqrt{g R \tan \theta}$
$=\sqrt{9.8 \times 300 \times \tan 15^{\circ}}=28.1 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$
and $v_{\max }=\sqrt{\dfrac{g R\left(\mu_{s}+\tan 15^{\circ}\right)}{1-\mu_{s} \tan 15^{\circ}}}$
$=\dfrac{9.8 \times 300(0.2+2-\sqrt{3})}{1-0.2(2-\sqrt{3})}$
$ = 38.1\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A right circular cone is fixed with its axis vertical and vertex down. A bead is in contact with its smooth inside surface and describes circular motion in a horizontal plane at the height of  $10\,cm$ above the vertex. Find its velocity in ${{m} / {s}}$. (Take ${\left.g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}\right)}$
explanation:
The Forces that act on the bead are shown (w.r.to bead) in the figure.
$N\cos \theta = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$N\sin \theta = mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
By dividing eqs. (2) and (1) we get
${\tan \theta=\dfrac{g r}{v^{2}}=\dfrac{r}{h}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951332/preptracker/questions/q_34730_explanation_1764951331967.png ${\Rightarrow v=\sqrt{g h}=\sqrt{10 \times \dfrac{10}{100}}=1 {~m} / {s}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A stone of mass $250\,g$ is tied to the end of a string of length $1.0\,m$. It is whirled in a horizontal circle on a smooth plane with a frequency of  $30{\text{ }}rev/\min .$  What is the tension in the string?
explanation:
Mass of the stone ${=250 {~g}=\dfrac{250 {~kg}}{1000}=\dfrac{1}{4} {~kg}}$
${T=m \omega^{2} r=\dfrac{1}{4} \times\left(\dfrac{2 \pi \times 30}{60}\right)^{2} \times 1=\dfrac{\pi^{2}}{4} N}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A car starts from rest with a constant tangential acceleration ${a_{0}}$ in a circular path of radius ${r}$. At time ${t_{0}}$ the car skids, find the value of coefficient of friction.
explanation:
The tangential and centripetal acceleration is provided only by the frictional force.
${a_{t}=a_{0}=\dfrac{d v}{d t}}$
${v=a_{0} t}$
The frictional force at time ${t}$ is
$f=m \sqrt{a_{t}^{2}+a_{r}^{2}}=m \sqrt{a_{0}^{2}+\dfrac{\left(a_{0}^{2}+t^{2}\right)^{2}}{r^{2}}}$
At ${t=t_{0}}$
${\Rightarrow m a_{0} \sqrt{1+\dfrac{a_{0}^{2} t_{0}^{4}}{r^{2}}}=f_{\text {max }}=\mu m g}$
(since the car skids beyound this speed)
${\mu m g=m a_{0} \sqrt{1+\dfrac{a_{0}^{2} t_{0}^{4}}{r^{2}}} \Rightarrow \mu=\dfrac{a_{0}}{g} \sqrt{1+\dfrac{a_{0}^{2} t_{0}^{4}}{r^{2}}}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A bob is whirled in a horizontal plane by means of a string with an initial speed of ${\omega}$  $rpm.$ The tension in the string is ${T}$. If speed becomes ${2 \omega}$ while keeping the same radius, the tension in the string becomes:
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951311/preptracker/questions/q_34674_explanation_1764951310959.png
$T = m\,\ell {\omega ^2}$
when angular velocity is doubled
$T' = m\,\ell \,{(2\omega )^2}$
$T' = 4T$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A stone of mass $1\,kg$ is tied to end of a massless string of length $1\,m$. If the breaking tension of the string is $400\,N$, then maximum linear velocity, the stone can have without breaking the string, while rotating in horizontal plane, is
explanation:
Here, ${T_{\max }} = 400\,N,l = 1\,m,\,m = 1\,kg$
$T_{\max }=$ Centripetal force.
As, $T_{\max }=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{l}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951301/preptracker/questions/q_34650_explanation_1764951300779.png $ \Rightarrow {v_{\max }} = \sqrt {\frac{{{T_{\max }}l}}{m}} $
$ \Rightarrow {v_{\max }} = \sqrt {\frac{{400 \times 1}}{1}} = 20\,m/s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A body is moving with constant speed, in a circle of radius $10\,m$. The body completes one revolution in $4\,s$. At the end of ${3^{rd{\rm{ }}}}$ second, the displacement of body (in $m$ ) from its starting point is
explanation:
Angular velocity $\omega = \frac{{2\pi }}{4}rad/s$
$ = \frac{\pi }{2}rad/s$
Angular displacement in $t = 3\,s$ is
$\theta = \omega t = \frac{\pi }{2} \times 3\,rad$.
So after $3\,s$, the particle will be at position $B$.
$\therefore$ Displacement
$ = AB = \sqrt {O{A^2} + O{B^2}} = \sqrt 2 R = 10\sqrt 2 \,m$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951299/preptracker/questions/q_34642_explanation_1764951298801.png
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A small block of mass $100\,g$ is tied to a spring of spring constant $7.5\,N{\rm{/}}m$ and length $20\,cm$. The other end of spring fixed at a particular point $A$. If the block moves in a circular path on a smooth horizontal surface with constant angular velocity $5\,rad{\rm{/}}s$ about point $A$, then tension in the spring is
explanation:
Mass of block, $m = 100\,g = 0.1\,kg$.
Spring constant, $k = 7.5\,N/m$;
Length, $x = 20\,cm = 0.2\,m = $ Elongation of spring.
Angular velocity, $\omega = 5\,rad/s$
As the block moves in circular path its centripetal force is given by, $F_{c}=m \omega^{2}(r+x)$
Spring force $\left(F_{s}\right)=$ Centripetal force $\left(F_{c}\right)$
$\Rightarrow k x=m \omega^{2}(r+x)$
$\Rightarrow 7.5 x=2.5(0.2+x)$
$\Rightarrow x=\dfrac{0.5}{5}=0.1$
Tension in spring $=k x=0.75 N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A coin placed on a rotating table just slips when it is placed at a distance of $1\,cm$ from the center If the angular velocity of the table is halved, it will just slip when placed at a distance of ____  from the centre.
explanation:
Static friction, $f_{s \max }=\mu m g=m \omega^{2} R \Rightarrow R=\dfrac{\mu g}{\omega^{2}}$
$\therefore \frac{{{R^\prime }}}{R} = {\left( {\frac{\omega }{{{\omega ^\prime }}}} \right)^2} = {\left( {\frac{\omega }{{\omega /2}}} \right)^2} = 4$
$ \Rightarrow {R^\prime } = 4\,cm$.
So distance from the center will be $4\,cm$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A child of mass $5\,kg$ is going round a merry go-round that makes 1 rotation in  $3.14\,s.$  The radius of the merry go-round is $2\,m$. The centrifugal force on the child will be
explanation:
Mass of child $ = 5\,kg$,
Radius of merry-go-round $ = 2{\mkern 1mu} \,m$
Angular velocity $\omega=\dfrac{2 \pi}{T}=\dfrac{2 \times 3.14}{3.14}$
$F_{c}=m \omega^{2} R$
$ \Rightarrow \omega = 2\,rad{\rm{/}}s$
$\therefore F_{C}=$ Contribugal Force
$=m \omega^{2} R$
$ = 5 \times {(2)^2} \times 2\,N = 40\,N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: An object moves at a constant speed along a circular path in a horizontal plane with center at the origin. When the object is at $x =  + 2\,m$, its velocity is $ - 4\hat j\,m/s$. The object's velocity ($v$) and acceleration $(a)$ at $x =  - 2\,m$ will be
explanation:
Given: $x=2 m, v=4 \hat{j} m / s \quad x=-2 m$
The object must be moving clock wise.
At $B(-2,0)$ the velocity is upward and magnitude is same, so $\vec v = + 4\hat j\,m{\rm{/}}s$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951265/preptracker/questions/q_34565_explanation_1764951264924.png Acceleration is always towards the centre,
$a = \frac{{{v^2}}}{r}\hat i = \frac{{4 \times 4}}{2} = 8\hat i\,m/{s^2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A car is moving on a horizontal curved road with radius $50\,m$. The approximate maximum speed of car will be, if friction between tyres and road is 0.34. [ take ${g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}}$]
explanation:
A car moving on a horizontal curved road with radius $(r) = 50\,m$
Friction between tyres and road
$(\mu)=0.34$
$ = \frac{{m{V_{{{\max }^2}}}}}{r}\mu \,mg$.
$0.34 = \frac{{{V_{{{\max }^2}}}}}{{50 \times 10}}{\rm{or}}\,\,v = 13\,m{\rm{/}}s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A car is moving with a constant speed of $20\,m{\rm{/}}s$ in a circular horizontal track of radius $40\,m$. A bob is suspended from the roof of the car by a massless string. The angle made by the string with the vertical will be (Take ${g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}}$)
explanation:
Speed, $v = 20\,m{\rm{/}}s$,
radius, $r = 40\,m,\,\,g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$
Let the tension in the string is $T$.
So, $T\sin \theta = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$T\cos \theta = mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
On dividing (1) by (2), we get
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{T \sin \theta}{T \cos \theta}=\dfrac{v^{2}}{r g}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955742/preptracker/questions/q_34545_explanation_1764955742026.png
or $\tan \theta=\dfrac{20 \times 20}{40 \times 10}=1$
$\therefore \phi=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ radian
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A vehicle of mass $200\,kg$ is moving along a levelled curved road of radius $70\,m$ with angular velocity of $0.2\,rad{\rm{/}}s$. The centripetal force acting on the vehicle is
explanation:
Mass of vehicle, $m = 200\,kg$,
Radius of curved road, $r = 70\,m$ and $\omega = 0.2\,rad{\rm{/}}s$.
Centripetal force acting on vehicle is, $m \omega^{2} r$
$F = 200 \times {(0.2)^2}70N$
$ \Rightarrow F = 560\,N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A body of mass $200\,g$ is tied to a spring of spring constant $12.5\,N{\rm{/}}m$, while the other end of spring is fixed at a point $O$. If the body moves about $O$ in a circular path on a smooth horizontal surface with constant angular speed. Then the ratio of extension in the spring to its natural length will be
explanation:
Given that, natural length of spring $=L$
Extension in the spring $=x$
Spring constant $k = 12.5\,N{\rm{/}}m$,
angular speed $\omega = 5\,rad{\rm{/}}s$
Here centripetal force is provided by spring force which is $K X$.
Centripetal force is,
$kx = m(L + x){\omega ^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Using eqn. (1), [ with ]
$\Rightarrow 12.5 x=\dfrac{1}{5}(L+x) 25 \Rightarrow 1.5 x=L$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{x}{L}=\dfrac{2}{3}$
So, correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A train is moving with a speed of $12\,m{\rm{/}}s$ on rails which are $1.5\,m$ apart. To negotiate a curve of radius $400\,m$, the height by which the outer rail should be raised with respect to the inner rail is
explanation:
Let $\theta$ is the angle of banking https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951255/preptracker/questions/q_34493_explanation_1764951255552.png $\tan \theta=\dfrac{v^{2}}{r g}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{H}{\sqrt{(1.5)^{2}-H^{2}}}=\dfrac{(12)^{2}}{400 \times 10}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} H = 0.054\,m \Rightarrow H = 5.4\,cm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question:
Assertion: Centripetal force is always required for motion in curved path.

Reason: On a banked curved track, vertical component of normal reaction provides the necessary centripetal.
explanation: When a vehicle takes a turn on a banked road, the necessary centripetal force is provided by the horizontal component of the normal reaction force, while the vertical component balances the weight of the vehicle. Roads are typically banked to accommodate vehicles traveling at average speeds. However, if a vehicle's speed is somewhat lower or higher than this average, the self-adjusting static friction between the tires and the road will come into play. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question:
Assertion: Improper banking of roads causes wear and tear of tyres.

Reason: The necessary centripetal force is provided by the force of friction between the tyres and the road.
explanation: The reason correctly explain assertion stating that the necessary centripetal force is provided by the force of friction between the tires and the road. If the road is improperly banked, the friction may not be sufficient to provide the required centripetal force, leading to increased wear and tear on the tires. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question:
Assertion: When an automobile while going too fast around a curve overturns, its inner wheels leave the ground first.

Reason: For a safe turn the velocity of automobile should be less than the value of safe limit velocity.
explanation:
Outer wheels have a longer distance to travel when going around the curve, which can cause the inner wheels to lift off the ground due to the centrifugal force acting on the vehicle.
$\Rightarrow$ Assertion is TRUE (correct)
To safely navigate a curve, the velocity of the automobile should be within the limits defined by the safe limit velocity for that particular curve.
$\Rightarrow$ Reason is TRUE (correct)
However, the reason does not directly explain assertion. So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question:
Assertion: As the frictional force increases, the safe velocity limit for taking a turn on an unbanked road also increases.

Reason: Banking of roads will increase the value of limiting (maximum) velocity.
explanation:
Given statements are different facts.For 'Assertion' to be TRUE (correct) $v_{\text {safe }}=\sqrt{\mu r g}$ for unbanked road
$\left(\right.$ since $\left.\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}=\mu m g\right)$
$\Rightarrow$ As $\mu$ increases, $v_{\text {safe }}$ also increases
For 'Reason' to be TRUE (correct)
$v_{\text {max }}=\sqrt{\dfrac{(\tan \theta+\mu) g r}{(1-\mu \tan \theta)}}$
$\Rightarrow$ As $\theta$ increases (banking), $v_{\max }$ also increases. So correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question:
Assertion: When a vehicle takes a turn on the road, it travels along a nearly circular path.

Reason: In circular motion, velocity of vehicle remains same.
explanation: In circular motion, the frictional force acts toward the center of the horizontal circular path, providing the necessary centripetal force to prevent the vehicle from overturning. This is because in circular motion, there is a change in the direction of motion, leading to a change in velocity. So correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question:
Assertion: During a turn, the value of centripetal force should be less than the limiting frictional force.

Reason: The centripetal force is provided by the frictional force between the tyres and the road.
explanation:
A body is capable of moving in a circular path due to the presence of a centripetal force. In the case of a vehicle, this centripetal force is provided by frictional force. Therefore, if the value of the frictional force ( $\mu m g$ ) is less than the centripetal force required, a vehicle cannot make a turn, and the bicycle would topple over. The condition to prevent overturning of the vehicle is given by $\mu m g \geq \dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}$.
So correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question:
Assertion: A cyclist always bends inwards while negotiating a curve.

Reason: By bending, cyclist lowers his centre of gravity
explanation:
The cyclist actually leans inward at an angle $\theta$ to generate the necessary force for circular motion. If $N$ represents the 'Normal reaction " force from the ground, it can be divided into two components: horizontal and vertical. The vertical component, $N \cos \theta$, counter balances the cyclist's weight (mg), while the horizontal component, $N \sin \theta$, supplies the required centripetal force for circular motion.
$\Rightarrow$ Assertion is correct
As his head is now lowered the position of centre of gravity shifts towards ground slightly.
Reason is correct but is not direct explanation for assertion. So correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A ball of mass $(m)0.5\,kg$ is attached to the end of a string having length $(L)0.5\,m$. The ball is rotated on a horizontal circular path about vertical axis. The maximum tension that the string can bear is $324\,N$. The maximum possible value of angular velocity of ball (in radian/s) is
explanation:
From figure, $R=L \sin \theta$ the horizontal component of tension $T$ provides the necessary centripetal force. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955725/preptracker/questions/q_34290_explanation_1764955724626.png $\therefore T \sin \theta=m \omega^{2} R$
$T \sin \theta=m \omega^{2} L \sin \theta$
$T=m \omega^{2} L$ or $\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{m L}}$
Substituting the given values, we get
$\omega = \sqrt {\frac{{324}}{{0.5 \times 0.5}}} = 36\,rad{\rm{/}}s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A body moves along a circular path of radius 10 $m$ and the coefficient of friction is 0.5. What should be its angular speed in $rad$/$s$ if it is not to slip from the surface $\left( {g = 9.8\,m{s^2}} \right)$?
explanation:
For moving on circular path without slipping, centrifugal force must be equal to frictional force.
That is,
$ \Rightarrow mr\,{\omega ^2} = \mu \,m\,g\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left[ {\therefore v = r\omega } \right]$
$ \Rightarrow r\,{\omega ^2} = \mu \,\,g$
$\therefore \omega = \sqrt {\frac{{\mu \,g}}{r}} = \sqrt {\frac{{0.5 \times 9.8}}{{10}}} = \,0.7\,rad/s.\,$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A car sometimes overturns while taking a turn. When  it overturns, it is:

options.text: The inner wheel which leaves the ground first
The outer wheel which leaves the ground first
Both the wheels leave the ground simultaneously
Either wheel which leaves the ground first

subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A car moving on a horizontal road may be thrown out of the road in taking a turn:

options.text: Due to the lack of proper centripetal force
Due to the rolling frictional force between the  type and road
Due to the reaction of the ground

subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A circular race track of radius 300 $m$ is banked at an angle of ${\rm{15}}^\circ $ . If the coefficient of friction between the wheels of a race car and the road is 0.2, what is the maximum permissible speed to avoid slipping?  (Take ${\rm{tan15}}^\circ  = 0.27$)
explanation:
The forces that act on the car are shown in the figure. As the car has tendency to slip upwards the frictional force acts down the incline.
At the verge of slipping
$mg + \mu N\sin \theta = N\cos \theta $
$\frac{{m{v^2}}}{R} = N\sin \theta + \mu N\cos \theta $
where $f = \mu N$
By dividing the above two equations
$v = \sqrt {gR\frac{{\left[ {\sin \theta + \mu \cos \theta } \right]}}{{\left[ {\cos \theta - \mu \sin \theta } \right]}}} = \sqrt {gR\frac{{\left[ {\mu + \tan \theta } \right]}}{{1 - \mu \tan \theta }}} $
$ = 38.2\,m{\rm{/}}s$
subject: Physics

question: The outer rail of the curve railway track is raised above the inner one:

question: The maximum speed that can be achieved without skidding by a car on a circular unbanked road of radius $R$ and coefficient of static friction  $\mu $, is
explanation:
In the given condition the required centripetal force is provided by frictional force between the road and the tyre.
$\frac{{m{v^2}}}{R} = \mu mg\quad \therefore \,\,\,v = \sqrt {\mu Rg} $
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A ring of mass $m$ moves from point 1 to point 2 along a smooth rigid horizontal wire with a constant speed $v$. The average force acting on the ring over the time of its motion from 1 to 2 is
explanation:
${F_{avg}} = \frac{{\Delta P}}{{\Delta t}}$
$\Delta \,t = \frac{{R\theta }}{v}$
${\overrightarrow p _i} = - mv\sin \frac{\theta }{2}\hat i - mv\cos \frac{\theta }{2}\hat j$
${\overrightarrow p _{\rm{f}}} = mv\sin \frac{\theta }{2}\hat i - mv\cos \frac{\theta }{2}\hat j$
$\Delta \overrightarrow p = {\overrightarrow p _{\rm{f}}} - {\overrightarrow p _i} = 2mv\sin \left( {\frac{\theta }{2}} \right)\hat i$
${F_{avg}} = \frac{{2m{v^2}\sin \frac{\theta }{2}}}{{R\theta }}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A bead $A$ can slide freely along a smooth rod bent in the form of a half circle of Radius $R$. The system is set in rotation with a constant angular velocity  $\omega $ about a vertical axis $OO$’. Find the angle $\theta $ corresponding to steady position of the bead.
explanation:
Net force acting along the tangent is
$mg\sin \theta - mr{\omega ^2}\cos \theta = 0$
or $mg\sin \theta \left[ {1 - \frac{{r{\omega ^2}}}{g}\cos \theta } \right] = 0$
or $\theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{g}{{R{\omega ^2}}}} \right)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A truck is carry a box of mass  $m = 50\,kg$ on its flat horizontal rough surface with coefficient of friction $\mu  = 0.3$. It is crossing a circular track of radius 27 $m$. What is the maximum speed of the truck so that the box does not slide from the truck while moving on the circular path?
explanation:
F.B.D. of the box in the truck is shown in the figure.
Here $\frac{{m{v^2}}}{R} = \mu \,mg$
${v^2} = \mu \,g\,R = 0.3 \times 10 \times 27$
$v = 32.4\,km/hr$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A hemispherical bowl of radius $R$ is rotated about its axis of symmetry which is kept vertical with angular velocity $\omega $. A small block is kept in the bowl. It remains stationary relative to the bowl surface at a position where the radius makes an angle  $\theta $ with the vertical. The friction is absent. The value of $\theta $ is :-
explanation:
Let $m$ be the mass of the particle. The centrifugal force acting on the particle is $mr{\omega ^2}$
As the particle is at rest w.r. to the bowl.
$N\cos \theta = mg$
$N\sin \theta = mr{\omega ^2}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\tan \theta = \frac{{r{\omega ^2}}}{g}$
$\frac{{\sin \theta }}{{\cos \theta }} = \frac{{(R\sin \theta ){\omega ^2}}}{g} \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac{g}{{R{\omega ^2}}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A block of mass $m$ is projected on a smooth horizontal circular track with velocity $v$. What is the average normal force exerted by the circular walls on the block during its motion from $A$ to $B$?
explanation:
Change in linear momentum of block
$\left| {\Delta p} \right| = 2mv$
Time taken to move from $A$ to $B$,
$\Delta t = \frac{{\pi R}}{v}$
Force exerted on the block $ = \frac{{2mv}}{{\Delta t}} = \frac{{2m{v^2}}}{{\pi R}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A small ring $P$ is threaded on a smooth wire bent in the form of a circle of radius $a$ and center $O$. The wire is rotating with constant angular speed $\omega $ about a vertical diameter $XY$, while the ring remains at rest relative to the wire at a distance $a$/2 from $XY$. Then ${\omega ^2}$ is equal to
explanation:
From the figure
$N\cos \theta = mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$N\sin \theta = \frac{{m{\omega ^2}a}}{2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From eqs. (1) & (2),
$\tan \theta = \frac{{{\omega ^2}a}}{{2g}} \Rightarrow {\omega ^2} = \frac{{2g\tan \theta }}{a}$
Now, $\sin \theta = \frac{a}{2} \times \frac{1}{a} = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \theta = {30^0}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\omega ^2} = \frac{{2g}}{{a\sqrt 3 }}$

explanation: As car is moving in anticlockwise direction and have tangential acceleration as well as radial acceleration. Friction component should be along tangential and radial acceleration. The correct option is (3).

question:
Study the following statements
I. When a stone is rotated at greater speed in circular path, greater force is required.
II. When a stone is rotated in a circle of smaller radius, greater force is required
III. When stone is rotated at greater speed in a circle of smaller radius, smaller force is required.
Which of the following statement($s$) is/are correct?
explanation:
The centripetal force on a rotating stone in a circle is
$F = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r}$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,F \propto {v^2},F \propto \frac{1}{r}$
So option (1) is correct.

question:
On a merry-go around, all parts of our body are subjected to an inward force.
We have feeling of being pushed outward in the direction of impending motion.
explanation:
In circular motion there is always an inward force (real force) acts on the body whereas the bodies feel outward force (pseudo force) or centrifugal force) on it.
Both statements are true. So option (3) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question:
Assertion: Force is required to move a body uniformly along a circle.

Reason: When a body is in uniform circular motion then its acceleration is zero.
explanation: When a body is moving in a circle, its speed remains same but velocity changes due to change in the direction of motion of body. As in circular motion the direction of velocity of body is changing so the acceleration cannot be zero.

options.text: Both assertion and reason are correct and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
Both assertion and reason are correct but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
Assertion is correct but reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question:
Assertion: Linear momentum of a body changes even when it is moving uniformly in a circle.

Reason: In uniform circular motion velocity remains constant.
explanation:
In uniform circular motion, the direction of motion changes, therefore velocity changes.
As $P = mv$ therefore momentum of a body also changes in uniform circular motion.

options.text: Both assertion and reason are correct and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
Both assertion and reason are correct but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
Assertion is correct but reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: In the given figure, $a = 15\,\,{\rm{m/}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}$ represents the total acceleration of a particle moving in the clockwise direction in a circle of radius $R = 2.5\,m$ at a given instant of time. The speed of the particle is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950025/preptracker/questions/q_33146_explanation_1764950025531.png From the figure
$\frac{{{a_t}}}{{{a_r}}} = \tan \left( {30^\circ } \right) = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}$
$\sqrt {a_t^2 + a_r^2} = 15$
$\sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{{{a_r}}}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)}^2} + a_r^2} = 15$
${a_r} = 15\sqrt {\frac{3}{4}} = \frac{{{v^2}}}{r}$
$v = \sqrt {15 \times 2.5\sqrt {\frac{3}{4}} } = 5.7m/s$
subject: Physics

question:
One end of a string of length $l$ is connected to a particle of mass $m$ and the other to a small peg on a smooth horizontal table. If the particle moves in a circle with speed $v$, the net force on the particle (directed towards the centre) is
($T$ is the tension in the string)
explanation: The necessary centripetal force needed for the particle to undergo circular motion is provided by the tension in the string i.e., $T.$

question: A circular race track of radius 300 $m$ is banked at an angle of $15^\circ $ . If the coefficient of friction between the wheels of a race car and the road is 0.2, what is the maximum permissible speed to avoid slipping?  (Take  $\tan 15^\circ  = 0.27$ )
explanation:
The forces that act on the car are shown in the figure. As the car has tendency to slip upwards the frictional force acts down the incline. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950012/preptracker/questions/q_33099_explanation_1764950012267.png At the verge of slipping
$mg + \mu N\sin \theta = N\cos \theta $
$\frac{{m{v^2}}}{R} = N\sin \theta + \mu N\cos \theta $
where $f = \mu N$
By dividing the above two equations
$v = \sqrt {gR\frac{{\left[ {\sin \theta + \mu \cos \theta } \right]}}{{\left[ {\cos \theta - \mu \sin \theta } \right]}}} = \sqrt {gR\frac{{\left[ {\mu + \tan \theta } \right]}}{{1 - \mu \tan \theta }}} $
$ = 38.2\,m{\rm{/}}s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A child starts running from rest along a circular track of radius $r$ with constant tangential acceleration $a$.  After time $t$ he feels that slipping of shoes on the ground has started. The coefficient of friction between shoes and the ground is[ $g$ $=$ acceleration due to gravity]
explanation:
When child moves in a circular track, he is acted upon by two forces as shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950010/preptracker/questions/q_33087_explanation_1764950009589.png Here, ${f_c} = f\sin \theta $ and ${f_t} = f\cos \theta $
where, ${f_c}$ is the centripetal force, ${f_t}$ is the tangential force, and is total resultant force.
Now, ${f_c} = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} = f\sin \theta $
and ${f_t} = ma = f\cos \theta $
Therefore, resultant force,
${f_{}} = \sqrt {{{\left( {f\sin \theta } \right)}^2} + {{\left( {f\cos \theta } \right)}^2}} $
$ = \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{{m{v^2}}}{r}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {ma} \right)}^2}} $
Also, when the shoes start slipping, the friction force becomes equal to resultant force
$\therefore {f_\mu } = {f_{}}$
$\mu mg = \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{{m{v^2}}}{r}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {ma} \right)}^2}} $
$ \Rightarrow {\mu ^2}{m^2}{g^2} = {\left( {\frac{{m{v^2}}}{r}} \right)^2} + {\left( {ma} \right)^2}$
$ \Rightarrow {\mu ^2}{g^2} = \frac{{{{\left( {at} \right)}^4} + {a^2}{r^2}}}{{{r^2}}}\left( {\because a = \frac{v}{t}} \right)$
or $\mu = \frac{{{{\left( {{a^4}{t^4} + {a^2}{r^2}} \right)}^{\frac{1}{2}}}}}{{gr}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Non-uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question:
A person is driving a vehicle at uniform speed of $5\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ on a level curved track of radius 5 $m$. The coefficient of static friction between tyres and road is 0.1. Will the person slip while taking the turn with the same speed ? Take $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$.
Choose the correct statement.
explanation:
The person will not slip if ${v^2} \le {m_s}Rg$
Here, ${m_s} = 0.1,R = 5\,m,g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
$ \Rightarrow {m_s}Rg = 5\,{m^2}{s^{ - 2}}$
$ \Rightarrow {v^2} < 5\,{m^2}{s^{ - 2}}$
Therefore the person will slip ${v^2} > 5\,{m^2}{s^{ - 2}}$

options.text: A person will not slip if  ${v^2} > 10{\mkern 1mu} {m^2}{s^{ - 2}}$

subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question: A block of mass 10 $kg$ is in contact against the inner wall of a hollow cylindrical drum of radius 1 $m$. The coefficient of friction between the block and the inner wall of the cylinder is 0.1. The minimum angular velocity needed for the cylinder to keep the block stationary when the cylinder is vertical and rotating about its axis, will be : ($g = 10\,m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949978/preptracker/questions/q_32935_explanation_1764949978243.png For equilibrium of the block friction
$f \ge mg$ and $N = mr{\omega ^2}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\mu N \ge mg$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\mu mr{\omega ^2} \ge mg$
$\omega \ge \sqrt {\frac{g}{{r\mu }}} $
${\omega _{\min }} = \sqrt {\frac{g}{{r\mu }}} $
${\omega _{\min }} = \sqrt {\frac{{10}}{{0.1 \times 1}}} = 10\,{\rm{rad/s}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion and friction in banking

question:
In the pulley system, the mass of ball is 1.2 times greater than the mass of the rod. The length of the rod is $50\,cm$. The ball is set on the same level as the lower end of the rod and then released. What is the acceleration of the rod with which it comes down?
Assume the pulleys and threads are massless and friction force is neglected. (Use, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$)
explanation:
The free body diagram for given system is as shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952189/preptracker/questions/q_107582_explanation_1764952188924.png For ball, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952190/preptracker/questions/q_107582_explanation_1764952189629.png $ \Rightarrow m{a_1} = 2T - mg{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
For rod, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952190/preptracker/questions/q_107582_explanation_1764952190255.png $ \Rightarrow M{a_2} = Mg - T\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
As, thread is uniform and of constant length, so $2 a_{1}=a_{2}$ From Eq. (1), we get
$\frac{{m{a_2}}}{2} = 2T - mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
Multiplying Eq. (2) by 2 and adding to Eq. (3), we get
$2 M a_{2}+\dfrac{m a_{2}}{2}=2 M g-m g$
$\Rightarrow a_{2}=\dfrac{(2 M-m)}{\left(2 M+\dfrac{m}{2}\right)} g=\dfrac{\left(2-\dfrac{m}{M}\right)}{\left(2+\dfrac{m}{2 M}\right)^{2}} g$
Here, $\dfrac{m}{M}=1.2$ and $g=10 {~ms}^{-2}$
$\therefore a_{2}=\dfrac{(2-1.2)}{\left(2+\dfrac{1.2}{2}\right)} \times 10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
$=\dfrac{0.8}{2.6} \times 10=\dfrac{4}{1.3} \simeq 3 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pulley Constraints without Fixed Frames

question: In the pulley system shown in the figure, the mass of $A$ is half of that of $\operatorname{rod} B$. The mass of $\operatorname{rod} B$ is same as the mass of block $C$. The rod length is $500\,cm$. The mass of pulleys and the threads may be neglected. The mass $A$ is set at the same level as the lower end of the rod and then released. After releasing the mass $A$, it would reach the top end of the $\operatorname{rod} B$ in time (Assume, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$)
explanation:
According to the question, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952205/preptracker/questions/q_107561_explanation_1764952205106.png $m_{A}=\dfrac{m_{B}}{2} \Rightarrow m_{B}=2 m_{A}$
and length of $\operatorname{rod}=500 {~cm}=5 {~m}$
Since, $\operatorname{rod} B$ and body $C$ is in equilibrium, $m_{B}=m_{C}$
By applying Newton's law of motion,
$2T - {m_A}g = {m_A}a\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
and $\left(m_{B}+m_{C}\right) g-2 T=\left(m_{B}+m_{C}\right) a$
or $2 m_{B} g-2 T=2 m_{B} a$
or $4 m_{A} g-2 T=4 m_{A} a$
Adding Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$3 m_{A} g=5 m_{A} a$
$\Rightarrow a=\dfrac{3}{5} g=\dfrac{3 \times 10}{5}=6 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
Now $a^{\prime}=$ acceleration of $A$ relative to
$B=a+a=2 a=12 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
If $t$ be the time to cross the rod of length $500 {~cm}=5 {~m}$
$\therefore$ From Eqs., $s=0+\dfrac{1}{2} a^{\prime} t^{2}$
$\Rightarrow t=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 s}{a^{\prime}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 5}{12}}=0.928 {~s} \approx 1 {~s}$
So,the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pulley Constraints without Fixed Frames

question: All surfaces shown in figure are assumed to be frictionless and the pulleys and the string are light. The acceleration of the block of mass  $2\,kg$  is
explanation:
For $4\,kg$ mass, $F B D$ is
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951510/preptracker/questions/q_35389_explanation_1764951509941.png
$ \Rightarrow {m_1}g - 2T = {m_1}{a_1}$ $\quad \quad (1)$
For $2\,kg$ mass, $FBD$ is
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951511/preptracker/questions/q_35389_explanation_1764951511243.png
$ \Rightarrow T - {m_2}g\,\sin \,\theta = {m_2}{a_2}$ $\quad \quad (2)$
For constrained motion
$\Sigma(T a \cos \theta)=0$
$-2 T a_{1}+T a_{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow a_{2}=2 a_{1}$
$T-m_{2} g \sin \theta=2 m_{2} a_{1}$ $\quad (3)$
Multiplying equation (3) by 2 and adding the result in equation (1), we have
$\begin{aligned}& m_{1} g-2 T+2 T-2 m_{2} g \sin \theta=m_{1} a_{1}+4 m_{2} a_{1} \\& \Rightarrow\left(m_{1} g-2 m_{2} g \sin \theta\right)=\left(m_{1}+4 m_{2}\right) a_{1}\end{aligned}$
Putting the values, we have
$\begin{aligned}& \left(4 \times g-2 \times 2 \times g \times \sin 30^{\circ}\right)=(4+4 \times 2) a_{1} \\& \Rightarrow a_{1}=\dfrac{2}{12} g=\dfrac{g}{6}\end{aligned}$
and $\Rightarrow a_{2}=2 a_{1}=\dfrac{g}{3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pulley Constraints with Fixed Frames

question: Block $A$ is given an acceleration $12 {~m} {~s}^{-2}$ towards left as shown in figure. Assuming block $B$ always remains horizontal, find the acceleration  of   $B$.
explanation:
$l_{1}+l_{2}=C \Rightarrow l_{1}^{\prime \prime}+l_{2}^{\prime \prime}=0$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951456/preptracker/questions/q_35266_explanation_1764951456149.png $ \Rightarrow \quad - {a_p} + \left( {12 - {a_p}} \right) = 0 \Rightarrow {a_p} = 6\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
$\quad {l_3} + {l_4} + {l_5} + {l_6} = C$
$ \Rightarrow \quad l_3^{\prime \prime } + l_4^{\prime \prime } + l_5^{\prime \prime } + l_6^{\prime \prime } = 0$
${a_P} - {a_B} - {a_B} - {a_B} = 0$
$ \Rightarrow {a_P} = 3\,\,{a_B} \Rightarrow {a_B} = 2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question:
In the arrangement shown, the pulleys are smooth and the strings are inextensible. Find the acceleration of block ${B}$ in
Take ${g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$
explanation:
Let acceleration of ${B}$ is a downwards using $F.B.D.$ of ${A}$ and ${B}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951411/preptracker/questions/q_35066_explanation_1764951409813.png $2T - mg = \frac{{ma}}{2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$mg - T = ma$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {\rm{ }}T = mg - ma\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From (1) and (2), we get
$2mg - 2ma - mg = \frac{{ma}}{2}$
$mg = \frac{{5ma}}{2} \Rightarrow a = \frac{{2g}}{5} = 4.00\,m/{s^2}$

question: For the situation shown in the figure, pulleys are light and smooth. Pulleys ${C}$ and ${D}$ are fixed, but ${A}$ and ${B}$ are movable. Find the value of ${m}$ (in $kg$) if ${m}$ is in equilibrium. (take, ${m_{1}=2 {~kg}}$ and ${m_{2}=1 {~kg}}$)
explanation:
For ${m_{2}}$,
$T - {m_2}g = {m_2}(2a)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Free body diagram $(FBD)$ of ${m_{2}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951397/preptracker/questions/q_35043_explanation_1764951397233.png For ${m_1}{m_1}g - 2{m_2} = {m_1}(a)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Free body diagram $(FBD)$ of ${m_{1}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951398/preptracker/questions/q_35043_explanation_1764951398253.png From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$\begin{aligned}& m_{1} g-2 m_{2} g=4 m_{2} a+m_{1} a \\\Rightarrow & a=0 {~m} / {s}^{2} \\\text { Or } & T=m_{2} g=10 {~N}\end{aligned}$
For ${m, 2 T=m g \Rightarrow m=\dfrac{20}{10}=2 {~kg}}$
Free body diagram of ${m}$
$\begin{aligned}& m g=2 T \\\Rightarrow \quad & m=\dfrac{2 \times 10}{10}=2 {~kg}\end{aligned}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951399/preptracker/questions/q_35043_explanation_1764951399112.png
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pulley Constraints with Fixed Frames

question:
In the arrangement shown, the ends $A$ and $B$ of an unstretchable string move downwards with uniform speed of $5\,m{\rm{/}}s.$ Pulleys $X$ and $Y$ are fixed. Mass $M$ moves upwards with a speed is
(Take $\sqrt{2}=1.41$ )
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951404/preptracker/questions/q_34999_explanation_1764951404325.png In right angled $\Delta MNO$, ${l^2} = {c^2} + {y^2}$
Differentiating w.r.t. time, we get,
$2l\frac{{dl}}{{dt}} = 0 + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{l}{y}\frac{{dl}}{{dt}}$
Now, $\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = {v_m};\frac{l}{y}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = \frac{1}{{\cos 45^\circ }};\frac{{dl}}{{dt}} = 5$
$\therefore \quad {v_m} = \frac{5}{{\cos 45^\circ }}$
$\therefore \quad {v_m} = 7.05\,m{\rm{/}}s$

question: In the figure shown the velocity of lift is $2\,m{\rm{/}}s$ while string is winding on the motor shaft with velocity $2\,m{\rm{/}}s$ and block $A$ is moving downwards with a velocity of $2\,m{\rm{/}}s$, then find out the velocity of block $B$

$4\,\,m{\rm{/}}s = {\vec v_{B,g}} - 2\,\,m{\rm{/}}s$
$ \Rightarrow {\vec v_{B,g}} = 6\,m{\rm{/}}s \uparrow $ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955809/preptracker/questions/q_34879_explanation_1764955809027.png

question: A block is dragged on a smooth plane with the help of a rope which moves with a velocity $v$ as shown in the figure. The horizontal velocity of the block is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955735/preptracker/questions/q_34514_explanation_1764955735242.png
$\begin{aligned}& r^{2}=x^{2}+H^{2} \\& \Rightarrow 2 r \dfrac{d r}{d t}=2 x \dfrac{d x}{d t}+0 \quad(\because H=\text { const })\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow v_{\text {horizontal }}=\dfrac{d x}{d t}$
$=\dfrac{r}{x} \dfrac{d r}{d x}=\dfrac{v}{\sin \theta}$

question: If the two blocks are in contact with each other, then find the relation between ${v_{1\,}}\,\,\& \,\,{v_2}$
explanation:
The two surfaces are as shown below.
${v_1}\,\cos \theta = {v_2}\cos \left( {90 - \theta } \right)$
${v_1}\,\cos \theta = {v_2}\,\sin \theta $
${v_1} = {v_2}\tan \theta $

explanation:

explanation:
The surfaces of rod and wedge that are in contact are as shown below.

question: If the rod $A$ and wedge $B$ are in contact then find the relation between ${v_A}$ & ${v_B}$
explanation:
The two surfaces which are in contact are shown below.
${v_A}\cos (90 - \theta ) = {v_B}\cos \theta $
The velocity components must be same along the normal.
${v_A}\sin \theta  = {v_B}\cos \theta $
$ \Rightarrow {v_B} = {v_A}{\mkern 1mu} \tan \theta .$

question: If the wedge is moving towards right side with velocity $u$ then find net velocity of block $A$.
explanation:
The positions of the wedge and block are expressed as shown in the figure.
The total length of the string is $l = 2x + y + c$
$\frac{{dl}}{{dt}} = 2\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} + \frac{{dc}}{{dt}}\quad \Rightarrow 0 = 2\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}$
Given, $\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = u$
$ \Rightarrow \left| {\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}} \right| = {V_{Ay}} = 2u$
As the block $A$ is fit in the wedge
${V_{Ax}} = u,{V_{Ax}}\& {V_{Ay}}$ are the $x$ and $y$ components of velocities of block $A$, w.r.to the ground.
${V_A} = \sqrt {{V_{Ax}}^2 + {V_{Ay}}^2} = \sqrt {{{(u)}^2} + {{(2u)}^2}} = \sqrt 5 u$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pulley Constraints with Fixed Frames

question: If the block and the wedge are in contact then 	find  the  condition in terms of velocities.
explanation:
The two surfaces which are in contact are shown below.
${v_2}$ component $=$ net ${v_1}$ component (along normal direction)
${v_2}\,\,\sin \,\theta = {v_{1y}}\,\cos \,\theta - {v_{1x}}\,\sin \theta $
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wedge Constraints

question: The accelerations of the two blocks are given then the acceleration of block $C$ is
explanation:
Express the position of moveable pulleys and blocks with respect to fixed frame as shown in the figure
String – 1:
${l_1} = {y_A} + {y_{{P_1}}} + {y_{{p_1}}} + c = {y_A} + 2{y_{{P_1}}} + c$
$0 = {V_A} + 2{V_{{p_1}}}$
$ \Rightarrow 0 = {a_A} + 2{a_{{p_1}}}\,\,\,\, \to \,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
String – 2:
${l_2} = ({y_B} - {y_{{p_2}}}) + ({y_C} - {y_{{p_2}}})$
${l_2} = {y_B} - 2{y_{{p_2}}} + {y_C}$
$0 = {V_B} - 2{V_{{p_2}}} + {V_C}$
$0 = {a_B} - 2{a_{{p_2}}} + {a_C}\,\,\,\, \to (2)$
From the diagram ${a_{{p_1}}} = {a_{{p_2}}}$
Since two pulleys are connected by a vertical string
From (1) and (2) we get
${a_B} + {a_C} + {a_A} = 0$
We assume an arbitrary sign convention.Take downward direction as $( + )ve$
Given ${a_A} = 5\,m/{s^2}$, ${a_B} = - 4m/{s^2}$
$ \Rightarrow {a_C} = - 1\,m/{s^2}$ i.e., block $C$ goes upward with $1\,m/{s^2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pulley Constraints with Fixed Frames

question: If block $B$ is moving towards right side with acceleration $b$. Find the acceleration of $A$ w.r.to ground.
explanation:
Let $x$ be the distance of block $B$ from rigid support and $y$ is the position of block $A$ w.r.to lower pulley connected to block $B$.The length of the string is $L = 4x + y + c$
By differentiating the equation w.r.to $t$ is
$0 = 4\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}\,\,\& \,\,0 = 4\frac{{{d^2}x}}{{d{t^2}}} + \frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{t^2}}}$
$\left| {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{t^2}}}} \right| = 4b = \left| {{a_{AB}}} \right|$
${a_A} = 4b$ (As the wedge $B$ is moving away from $A$ and block $A$ will lose contact with $B$ hence the horizontal component of acceleration of $A$ is zero. Since there is no horizontal force on the block $A$ at $t = 0$)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pulley Constraints with Fixed Frames

question: In the arrangement shown in the figure, if the 	acceleration of $B$ is a then the acceleration of $A$ is
explanation:
The block $B$ slides along the fixed inclined surface. Acceleration of $B$ makes an angle $90 - \alpha $ w.r.to vertical direction.The surfaces of $A$ and $B$ which are in contact are shown below.
Here $b$ is the acceleration of $A$
From the figure
$x + 90 - \theta + 90 - \alpha = 90 \Rightarrow x = (\alpha + \theta ) - 90$
As the two surfaces are in contact
$a\cos [(\alpha + \theta ) - 90] = b\cos (90 - \theta )$
$a\sin (\alpha + \theta ) = b\sin \theta $
$b = \frac{a}{{\sin \theta }}[\sin \alpha \cos \theta + \cos \alpha \sin \theta ]$
$b = a[\sin \alpha \cot \theta + \cos \alpha ]$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wedge Constraints

question: small block of mass $m$ rests on a  smooth wedge of angle  $\theta $. With what horizontal  acceleration a should the wedge be pulled, as shown in fig, so that the block falls freely (assume that the system is in earth gravitational field).
explanation:
The acceleration of two surfaces that are in contact are shown in figure.
$a\sin \theta = g\cos \theta $ (As the block falls freely)
$a = g\cot \theta $
subject: Physics

question: Two blocks are connected by an inextensible string. Find the relation between their accelerations.
explanation:
The positions of the two blocks are shown w.r.to the fixed reference frame.
Total length of the string is $l = 4y + 3x + c$
$\frac{{dl}}{{dt}} = 4\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} + 3\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + \frac{{dc}}{{dt}}$
$0 = 4\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} + 3\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 0$
$4{v_2} = 3{v_1} \Rightarrow 4{a_2} = 3{a_1}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pulley Constraints with Fixed Frames

question: Find the acceleration of block $A$.
explanation:
The positions of block $A$ and the pulley are shown in the figure.
The total length of the string is $l = x + y$
On differentiating the equation
$\left| {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{t^2}}}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{{d^2}x}}{{d{t^2}}}} \right|$
The acceleration of the wedge is $\frac{{{d^2}x}}{{d{t^2}}} = b$
The $y$-component of acceleration of the block is $ \Rightarrow \frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{t^2}}} = - b$ , the block has two components of accelerations. The net acceleration of block $A$ is
$\sqrt {{b^2} + {b^2}} = \sqrt 2 b$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Pulley Constraints with Fixed Frames

question: A block of mass $m$ is placed on a smooth inclined wedge $ABC$ of inclination $\theta $ as shown in the figure. The wedge is given an acceleration ‘$a$’ towards the right. The relation between $a$ and  $\theta $ for the block to remain stationary on the wedge is
explanation:
The Pseudo force ($ma$)on the block is shown in the figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950027/preptracker/questions/q_33152_explanation_1764950026956.png At equillibrium,
$ma\,\cos \theta = mg\,\sin \,\theta $
$a = g\tan \theta $

question: Match the conditions and concepts related to particle equilibrium in Column I with their correct descriptions or principles from Column II. Choose the correct answer using the codes given below.
explanation:
A matches with Q because the First Law of Motion states that a particle remains at rest or continues to move at a constant velocity unless acted upon by a net external force.
B matches with R because in equilibrium, the two forces acting on a particle must be equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.
C matches with S because if two forces are in equilibrium with a third force, the vector sum or the net force must be zero.
D matches with P because for three concurrent forces to be in equilibrium, their vector sum must be zero, which means that no net force is acting on the particle. Correct option is (4).

options.text: A - P,  B - Q,  C - R, D - S
A - R,  B - P,  C - S,  D - Q
A - Q,  B - R,  C - P,  D - S
A - Q,   B - R,  C - S,  D - P

subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of a Particle

question:
Read the ${\rm{Statement - A}}$ and ${\rm{Statement - B}}$ carefully to mark the correct option given below:
For a body in equilibrium under the action of forces, the forces can be represented by the sides of a closed polygon with arrows directed in the same sense.
The static frictional force is always equal to the kinetic frictional force.
explanation: Statement A is true as it describes the polygon method for representing forces in equilibrium. Statement B is false because the static frictional force can vary up to a maximum value, while kinetic friction is generally constant and less than the maximum static friction. Correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of a Particle

question:
Read the ${\rm{Statement - A}}$ and ${\rm{Statement - B}}$ carefully to mark the correct option given below:
If two forces act on a particle and the particle is in equilibrium, then these two forces must be equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.
A particle in equilibrium under two forces could be moving with a varying velocity.
explanation: Statement A is true as it is a condition for equilibrium that if only two forces act on a particle, they must be equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to have a net force of zero. Statement B is false because a particle in equilibrium cannot have a varying velocity; it must either be at rest or move with constant velocity. Correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of a Particle

question:
Read the ${\rm{Statement - A}}$ and ${\rm{Statement - B}}$ carefully to mark the correct option given below:
For a particle in equilibrium under the action of three concurrent forces, these forces must be equal in magnitude.
The forces acting on a particle in equilibrium can have different magnitudes if their vector sum is zero.
explanation: Statement A is false because the forces acting on a particle in equilibrium do not need to be equal in magnitude; they only need to add up to zero as a vector sum. Statement B is true as it correctly states that the forces can have different magnitudes as long as their vector sum equals zero. Correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of a Particle

question:
Read the ${\rm{Assertion}}$ and ${\rm{Reason}}$ carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: A particle remains in equilibrium if the vector sum of all the forces acting on it is zero.

Reason: When a particle is in equilibrium, it may either be at rest or move with constant velocity according to Newton's first law of motion.
explanation: The assertion accurately states the condition for the equilibrium of a particle where the sum of forces is zero, which implies no net force. The reason is correct and provides the correct explanation according to Newton's first law, which states that a body in a state of rest or uniform motion will remain so unless acted upon by a net external force. Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

subject: Physics
