question: A block is between two surfaces as shown in the figure. Find the normal reaction at both surfaces. [Assume, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ ]
explanation:
The given situation is shown in the figure.
Resolving applied force, we have following forces on blocks https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957820/preptracker/questions/q_107580_explanation_1764957819834.png Given,
$\tan \theta=\dfrac{3}{4} \sin \theta=\dfrac{3}{5}$ and $\cos \theta=\dfrac{4}{5}$
So, horizontal component of $F$,
$F_{1}=12 \times \cos \theta=12 \times \dfrac{4}{5}=\dfrac{48}{5} {~N}$
and vertical component,
$F_{2}=12 \times \sin \theta=12 \times \dfrac{3}{5}=\dfrac{36}{5} {~N}$
So, total applied force on ground is
$10+\dfrac{36}{5}+2 \times 10=37.2 {~N}$
So, reaction $N_{1}$ of ground $=37.2 N$ upwards Also, total force horizontally on wall is
$F_{1}=\dfrac{48}{5}=9.6 {~N}$
So, reaction $N_{2}$ of wall $=9.6 N$ towards left
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of a Particle

question: A box of mass $m$ is in equilibrium under the application of three forces as shown below. If the magnitude of $F_{1}$ is $10\,N$, what is the magnitude of $F_{3}$ ?
explanation:
All forces are resolved along two perpendicular axes ( $X$ and $Y$ ) as shown in the figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952225/preptracker/questions/q_107570_explanation_1764952224870.png The block of mass $m$ is in equilibrium.
Hence, resolving the forces in $X$-direction, we get
$\left|F_{2}\right| \cos \left(60^{\circ}\right)=\left|F_{1}\right| \cos \left(30^{\circ}\right)$
$\left|F_{2}\right| \times \dfrac{1}{2}=10 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \quad\left(\because\left|F_{1}\right|=10 N\right.$ given $)$
$\left|F_{2}\right|=10 \sqrt{3} N$
Again, resolving the force in $Y$-direction, we get
$\left|F_{3}\right|=\left|F_{1}\right| \sin \left(30^{\circ}\right)+\left|F_{2}\right| \sin \left(60^{\circ}\right)$
$=10 \times \dfrac{1}{2}+10 \sqrt{3} \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=5+15=20 {~N}$
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of a Particle

question: String $A B$ of unstretched length, $L$ is stretched by applying a force $F$ at the mid-point $C$ such that the segments $A C$ and $B C$ make an angle $\theta$ as shown in the figure. The string may be considered as an elastic element with a force to elongation ratio $K$. The force $F$ is given by
explanation:
Extension $x$ in $A C$ is
$x=L^{\prime} / 2-L / 2$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957805/preptracker/questions/q_107560_explanation_1764957804869.png $\Rightarrow x=\dfrac{L}{2 \cos \theta}-\dfrac{L}{2}=\dfrac{L}{2}\left(\dfrac{1-\cos \theta}{\cos \theta}\right)$
Given, $\dfrac{T}{x}=K$
$\therefore \quad T=\dfrac{L K}{2}\left(\dfrac{1-\cos \theta}{\cos \theta}\right)$
Using Lami's theorem,
$\dfrac{F}{\sin (\pi-2 \theta)}=\dfrac{T}{\sin \left(90^{\circ}+\theta\right)}$
$\dfrac{F}{\sin (\pi-2 \theta)}=\dfrac{\dfrac{L}{2} K\left(\dfrac{1-\cos \theta}{\cos \theta}\right)}{\sin \left(90^{\circ}+\theta\right)}$
$\Rightarrow F=\dfrac{K L}{2} \dfrac{(1-\cos \theta) \sin 2 \theta}{\cos ^{2} \theta}$
$=\dfrac{K L}{2} \dfrac{(1-\cos \theta) 2 \sin \theta \cos \theta}{\cos ^{2} \theta}$
$F=K L(1-\cos \theta) \cdot \tan \theta$
So,the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of a Particle

question: A  $1\,kg$  mass is suspended from the ceiling by a rope of length $4 m$. A horizontal force ' $F$ ' is applied at the mid point of the rope so that the rope makes an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with respect to the vertical axis as shown in figure. The magnitude of $F$ is (Assume that the system is in equilibrium and $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$ )
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955884/preptracker/questions/q_35405_explanation_1764955883874.png
${T_2} = mg = {T_1}\cos 45^\circ $ $\quad \quad (1)$
$F=T_{1} \sin 45^{\circ}$ $\quad \quad (2)$
Dividing equation (2) by (1), we get
$\frac{{{T_1}\sin 45^\circ }}{{{T_1}\cos 45^\circ }} = \frac{F}{{mg}}$
$ \Rightarrow 1 = \frac{F}{{mg}}$
$ \Rightarrow F = 10\,N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Lami’s Theorem

question: A body of weight $200\,N$ is suspended from a tree branch through a chain of mass $10\,kg$ . The branch pulls the chain by a force equal to (if $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$ )
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955881/preptracker/questions/q_35375_explanation_1764955880622.png
$F=10 g+200 N$
$ = 10 \times 10 + 200 = 300\,N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of a Particle

question: Three identical rigid circular cylinders ${A, B}$ and ${C}$ are placed on smooth inclined plane shown in the figure. The minimum value of ${\tan \theta}$ that prevent the arrangement from collapse is ${\dfrac{1}{3 \sqrt{n}}}$. The value of ${n}$ is
explanation:
From free body diagram of cylinder ${A, 2 N \cos 30^{\circ}=m g}$
${\therefore \quad N=\dfrac{m g}{\sqrt{3}}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951374/preptracker/questions/q_35031_explanation_1764951372756.png From free body diagram of cylinder ${B}$,
$\begin{aligned}& N_{0} \cos \theta={mg}+{N} \cos 30^{\circ}, \\& N_{0} \sin \theta={N} \sin 30^{\circ} \\& \tan \theta=\dfrac{N \sin 30^{\circ}}{m g+N \cos 30^{\circ}}\end{aligned}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951375/preptracker/questions/q_35031_explanation_1764951375212.png Putting the value of ${N, \tan \theta=\dfrac{1}{3 \sqrt{3}}}$
$\therefore \quad n=3$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of a Particle

question: A body of weight ${W_{1}}$ is suspended from the ceiling of a room through a chain of weight ${W_{2}}$. The ceiling pulls the chain by a force
explanation:
A body of weight$({W_1})$is suspended from a ceiling though a chain we weight $({W_2})$as shown in the figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951387/preptracker/questions/q_34895_explanation_1764951386764.png the free body diagram of the chain will be https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951387/preptracker/questions/q_34895_explanation_1764951387570.png Here,$N = $force by which the ceiling pulls the chain
Balancing forces we get
$N = {W_1} + {W_2}$.
So correct option is(3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of a Particle

question: A weight $w$ is suspended from the mid-point of a rope, whose ends are at the same level. In order to make the rope perfectly horizontal, the force applied to each of its ends must be
explanation:
For equilibrium of body, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955789/preptracker/questions/q_34781_explanation_1764955788697.png
$w = 2T\cos \theta $
$ \Rightarrow \quad T = \frac{w}{{2\cos \theta }}$
For the string to be horizontal,
$\theta = 90^\circ $
$\therefore \quad T = \frac{w}{{2\cos 90^\circ }} \Rightarrow T = \infty $
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of a Particle

question: A mass $M$ is hung with a light inextensible string as shown in the figure. Find the tension of the horizontal string.
explanation:
As there is a load at $P$, so tension in $A P$ and
$P B$ will be different. Let these be $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$
respectively. For vertical equilibrium of $P$, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951288/preptracker/questions/q_34769_explanation_1764951288286.png
${T_2}\cos 60^\circ = Mg\,\,i.e.,\,\,\quad {T_2} = 2Mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
and for horizontal equilibrium of $P$,
$T_{1}=T_{2} \sin 60^{\circ}=T_{2}(\sqrt{3} / 2)$
Substituting the value of $T_{2}$ from Eq. (1),
${T_1} = (2Mg) \times (\sqrt 3 /2) = \sqrt 3 Mg$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Lami’s Theorem

question: If the shown system is in equilibrium, then which of the following options is correct?
explanation:
The resolution of tensions are shown in the following figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951317/preptracker/questions/q_34699_explanation_1764951317242.png ${T_{3}=W_{2} \quad}$ and ${\quad T_{3}=T_{2}}$ (same rope)
${T_{1} \sin \theta=T_{2} \sin \theta \Rightarrow T_{1}=T_{2}}$
Hence, ${T_{1}=T_{2}=T_{3}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of a Particle

question: A block of mass ${m}$ is connected with two strings, as shown in the figure. The ratio of ${\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}}$ is
explanation:
The components of tension are shown in the figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955778/preptracker/questions/q_34695_explanation_1764955778133.png $\dfrac{\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{\dfrac{1}{2}}=\sqrt{3}$
As the block is in horizontal equilibrium
${T_{1} \cos 60^{\circ}=T_{2} \cos 45^{\circ}}$
${\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\dfrac{\cos 45^{\circ}}{\cos 60^{\circ}}=\dfrac{(1 / \sqrt{2})}{(1 / 2)} \sqrt{2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Lami’s Theorem

question: A block of $\sqrt 3 \,kg$ is attached to a string whose other end is attached to the wall. An unknown force $F$ is applied so that the string makes an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the wall. The tension $T$ is  (Given $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$)
explanation:
The block is in equilibrium under the action of forces $\overrightarrow{F g}, \vec{T}$ and $\vec{F}$. These forces must form a closed triangle. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951269/preptracker/questions/q_34581_explanation_1764951268884.png $\dfrac{T}{F g}=\dfrac{1}{\cos 30^{\circ}}$
$ \Rightarrow T = mg{\rm{/}}\cos \,30^\circ = 20\,N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Lami’s Theorem

question: An object of mass $8\,kg$ is hanging from one end of a uniform rod $C D$ of mass $2\,kg$ and length $1\,m$ pivoted at its end $C$ on a vertical wall as shown in figure. It is supported by a cable $A B$ such that the system is in equilibrium. The tension in the cable is (Take ${g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}}$)
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956853/preptracker/questions/q_34550_explanation_1764956852642.png The weight of rod acts at the mid point $G$ of the rod.
Now, let $T$ is the tension in the string.
Taking moment of all forces about $C$,
$T \sin 30^{\circ} \times 0.60=8 g \times 1+2 g \times 0.5$
$T = \frac{{9 \times 10 \times 2}}{{0.6}} = 300\,N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of a Particle

question: A steel wire can withstand a load upto $2940\,N$. A load of $150\,kg$ is suspended from a rigid support. The maximum angle through which the wire can be displaced from the mean position, so that the wire does not break when the load passes through the position of equilibrium, is
explanation:
While crossing the equilibrium position
$\begin{aligned}& T=m g+\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}=m g+\dfrac{m}{l}[2 g l(1-\cos \theta) \\& =m g+2 m g-2 m g \cos \theta=\mu g[3-2 \cos \theta] \\& \therefore 3-2 \cos \theta=\dfrac{2940}{(150)(9.8)}=2 \\& \Rightarrow 2 \cos \theta=1 \Rightarrow c s \theta=\dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \theta=60^{\circ}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Lami’s Theorem

question:
Assertion: A body subjected to three concurrent forces may be in equilibrium.

Reason: For equilibrium, the sum of all the concurrent forces acting at a point should be zero.
explanation: A body subjected to three concurrent forces may indeed be in equilibrium if the vector sum of these forces is zero. The reason correctly explains that for equilibrium, the sum of all the concurrent forces acting at a point should be zero. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: If large number of concurrent forces acting on the same point, then the point will be in equilibrium, if sum of all the forces is equal to zero.

Reason: Equilibrium of a particle in mechanics refers to the situation when the net external force on the particle is non zero.
explanation:
A set of forces is considered concurrent if all the lines of action if those forces all come (intersect) together at a single point $P$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955727/preptracker/questions/q_34361_explanation_1764955727223.png The conditions for equilibrium of $P$ are$\sum F_{x}=0, \sum F_{y}=0$
$\Rightarrow$ Assertion is true
$\Rightarrow$ Net acceleration will be zero. This is true whether the type of equilibrium is static or dynamic.
In case of 'static equilibrium', the point has zero velocity.
In case of 'dynamic equilibrium' the point has non-zero velocity
In mechanics, equilibrium corresponds to${\left( {{{\vec F}_{net{\rm{ }}}}} \right)_{external{\rm{ }}}} = 0$
$\Rightarrow$ Reason is false
So, correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: In the figure shown, the tension in the horizontal cord is $30 N$. The weight of the body $B$ is
explanation:
Let $T_{1}$ be the tension in cord 1 and $T_{2}$ be the tension in cord 2 respectively. Resolving $T_{2}$ into two rectangular components. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951240/preptracker/questions/q_34286_explanation_1764951239600.png In equilibrium,
${T_2}\sin 45^\circ = 30\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
${T_2}\cos 45^\circ = {T_1}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
${T_1} = W\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
From (1), we get ${T_2} = 30\sqrt 2 \,N$
On substituting this value of $T_{2}$ in (2), we get
$T_{1}=30 \sqrt{2} \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} N=30 N$
From (3), $W = {T_1} = 30\,N$
Hence, the weight of the body $B$ is $30{\rm{ }}N$.

$T\cos 45^\circ = {T_B}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} \left( 2 \right)$

question: A ball of $1\,kg$ hangs in equilibrium from two strings $O A$ and $O B$ as shown in figure. What are the tensions in strings $O A$ and $O B$ ? (Taking $g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$ )
explanation:
Various forces acting on the ball are shown in figure. Applying Lami's theorem,
$\dfrac{T_{1}}{\sin 150^{\circ}}=\dfrac{T_{2}}{\sin 120^{\circ}}=\dfrac{10}{\sin 90^{\circ}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951224/preptracker/questions/q_34230_explanation_1764951223079.png or $\dfrac{T_{1}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\dfrac{T_{2}}{\sin 60^{\circ}}=\dfrac{10}{1}$
$\therefore T_{1}=10 \sin 30^{\circ}=10 \times 0.5=5 \mathrm{~N}$
and $T_{2}=10 \sin 30^{\circ}=10 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=5 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~N}$

question: A boy of mass $40\,kg$ stands in a frame of mass $360\,kg$. He pulls on a light rope which passes over a pulley. The other end of the rope is firmly attached to the frame. What force should be exerted by the boy on the rope for the system to be in equilibrium?
explanation:
If $T$ is the tension in the rope, then the force exerted by the boy $B$ on the rope is equal to the force exerted by the rope on the boy. Let $R$ be the normal reaction between the boy and the frame.
The free body diagrams of boy and frame are shown in figures (a) and (b) respectively. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951219/preptracker/questions/q_34226_explanation_1764951218600.png From the free body diagram of boy
$T + R = 40\,g\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
From the free body diagram of frame
$T = R + 360\,g\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Adding (1) and (2), we get
$2T = 400\,g\,{\rm{or}}\,T = 200\,g$

question: In which of the following cases the net force acting on the body is not zero?
explanation: As the rain drop is falling down with a constant speed, its acceleration, $a = 0.$  Hence, net force on the drop is equal to zero. As the cork is floating on the surface of water, its weight is balanced by the upthrust. Hence, net force on the cork is zero.  As the car is moving with constant velocity, its acceleration  $a = 0.$Hence, net force on the car, $F = ma = 0.$ Whenever a body is thrown vertically upwards gravitational pull of earth gives it a uniform acceleration, $a = g$ in downward direction. Hence, net force on the pebble $ = mg$.

options.text: A drop of rain falling down with a constant speed
A cork of mass 10 $g$ floating down with a constant speed
A car moving with a constant speed of $20\,\,km\,{h^{ - 1}}$ on a rough road
A pebble of mass 0.05 $kg$ is thrown vertically  upwards

question: A block of mass 30 $kg$ is suspended by three strings as shown in fig. Find the tension in string $B$.
explanation:
Method I: The whole system is in equilibrium; therefore, for each part $\sum {\overrightarrow F = 0} $. From the free body diagram of block $C$, ${T_C} = 300\,N$
Now consider the equilibrium of point $O$,
$\sum {{F_x} = 0\,\,{\rm{or}}\,\,{T_B}} \cos 37^\circ - {T_A} = 0$
$\therefore {T_A} = {T_B}\cos 37^\circ = {T_B}.\frac{4}{5}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
${\mathop{\rm and}\nolimits} \,\,\sum {{F_y}} = 0\,\,or\,\,{T_C} = {T_B}\sin 37^\circ \,\,\,\,\,(2)$
$\therefore {T_B} = \frac{{{T_C}}}{{\sin 37^\circ }} = \frac{{300}}{{3/5}} = 500N$
$ \Rightarrow {T_A} = 400N$
Method II: Using Lami’s theorem, we have
$\frac{{{T_A}}}{{\sin (90 + 37^\circ )}} = \frac{{{T_B}}}{{\sin 90^\circ }} = \frac{{{T_C}}}{{\sin (180 - 37^\circ )}}$
We know that
${T_C} = 300N$
and ${T_B} = \frac{{{T_C}}}{{\sin 37^\circ }} = \frac{{300}}{{3/5}} = 500N$
${T_A} = {T_C}\left( {\frac{{\sin (90 + 37^\circ )}}{{\sin (180 - 37^\circ )}}} \right) = 300\left( {\frac{{\cos 37^\circ }}{{\sin 37^\circ }}} \right) = 400N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Lami’s Theorem

question:
The block is in equilibrium:
$(i)\,\,{T_1} = \frac{{mg\cos \beta }}{{\sin (\alpha + \beta )}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(ii)\,\,{T_2} = \frac{{mg\cos \alpha }}{{\sin (\alpha + \beta )}}$
$(iii)\,\,\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}} = \frac{{\cos \beta }}{{\cos \alpha }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(iv)\,\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}} = \frac{{\cos \alpha }}{{\cos \beta }}$

explanation:

$\frac{{{T_1}}}{{\cos \beta }} = \frac{{{T_2}}}{{\cos \alpha }} = \frac{{mg}}{{\sin (\alpha + \beta )}}$
${T_1} = \frac{{mg\cos \beta }}{{\sin (\alpha + \beta )}}$
${T_2} = \frac{{mg\cos \alpha }}{{\sin (\alpha + \beta )}}$

question: A body of mass 60 $kg$ suspended by means of three strings. $P$, $Q$ and $R$ as shown in the figure is in equilibrium. The tension in the string $P$ is:
explanation:
Method - 1
The free body diagram of mass $M$ is shown in figure. Let $T$ be the tension in the string $P$.
Taking component of forces
$R\cos \theta = Mg \Rightarrow R\cos {60^0} = Mg\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
${\mathop{\rm and}\nolimits} \,\,R\sin {60^0} = T\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
By eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$ \Rightarrow \tan 60^\circ = \frac{T}{{Mg}} \Rightarrow T = Mg\tan 60^\circ $
$ \Rightarrow T = 60 \times g \times \sqrt 3 = 103.9gN$
Method - 2
Using lami’s theorem
$\frac{R}{{\sin 90^\circ }} = \frac{P}{{\sin \left( {90^\circ + 30^\circ } \right)}} = \frac{{60g}}{{\sin \left( {90^\circ + 60^\circ } \right)}}$
$ \Rightarrow P = 103.9gN$

question: A block of mass $0.5\,kg$ is at rest on a horizontal table. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the table and the block is 0.2. explanation:
Here force applied
$F_{\text {app }}=5 N, m=0.5 {~kg}, \mu_{k}=0.2$
$a=\dfrac{F_{a p p}-f_{k}}{m}=\dfrac{F_{a p p}-\mu N}{m}$
or, $\dfrac{v-u}{t}=\dfrac{F_{\text {app }}-\mu_{k} m g}{m}$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957842/preptracker/questions/q_107680_explanation_1764957842062.png or, $\quad \dfrac{v-0}{4}=\dfrac{5-(0.2 \times 0.5 \times 10)}{0.5}$
$ \Rightarrow v = \frac{{5 - 1}}{{0.5}} \times 4 = 32\,m/s$
Therefore kinetic energy of the block in a time of $4\,s$
$ = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.5 \times {(32)^2} = 256\,J$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on a Horizontal/Vertical Surface

question: A block is kept on a rough horizontal surface. The acceleration of the block increases from $6\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ to $11\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ when the horizontal force acting on it increases from $20\,N$ to $30\,N$. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and the surface is (Acceleration due to gravity $ = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$)
explanation:
Acceleration of the block,
$a=\dfrac{F-f_{K}}{m}=\dfrac{F-\mu N}{m}=\dfrac{F-\mu m g}{m}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952258/preptracker/questions/q_107679_explanation_1764952257880.png $\therefore {\rm{ }}{a_1} = \frac{{{F_1} - \mu mg}}{m} \Rightarrow 6 = \frac{{20 - \mu mg}}{m}$
$ \Rightarrow 6 + \mu g = \frac{{20}}{m}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Also, $a_{2}=\dfrac{F_{2}-\mu m g}{m} \Rightarrow 11=\dfrac{30-\mu m g}{m}$
$ \Rightarrow 11 + \mu g = \frac{{30}}{m}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From eq (1) and (2), we get
$\dfrac{6+\mu g}{11+\mu g}=\dfrac{2}{3}$
$\Rightarrow 18+30 \mu=22+20 \mu$
$\Rightarrow 10 \mu=4$
$\mu=0.4$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on a Horizontal/Vertical Surface

question: Match the correct physical interpretations or examples related to forces Column I with the types of forces in Column II. Choose the correct option using the codes provided below.
explanation:
A matches with Q because the gravitational force can provide the necessary centripetal force for celestial bodies in orbit.
B matches with P because static friction is a self-adjusting force that resists the motion of objects until a certain limit.
C matches with S because kinetic friction is the force that acts when objects are in motion and is typically less than static friction.
D matches with R because normal reaction is the force exerted by a surface in direct contact with another object. Correct option is (1).

options.text: A - Q,     B - P,  C - S, D - R
A - P,  B - R, C - S,  D - Q
A - S,  B - Q,       C - P,  D - R
A - P,   B - S,  C - Q,  D - R

subject: Physics

question:
Read the ${\rm{Statement - A}}$ and ${\rm{Statement - B}}$ carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Kinetic friction depends on the velocity of the moving objects.
Rolling friction is typically smaller than static or sliding friction.
explanation: Statement A is false because kinetic friction is nearly independent of velocity. Statement B is true because rolling friction is much smaller compared to static or sliding friction. Correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question:
Read the ${\rm{Statement - A}}$ and ${\rm{Statement - B}}$ carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The gravitational force can act at a distance without the need for any intervening medium.
Friction is an example of a non-contact force.
explanation: Statement A is true as gravitational force is a long-range force that does not require a medium to act through. Statement B is false because friction is a contact force that arises due to the interaction of surfaces in contact. Correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question:
Read the ${\rm{Statement - A}}$ and ${\rm{Statement - B}}$ carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Frictional forces always do negative work since they oppose motion.
The work done by friction can be zero if there is no displacement of the object.
explanation: Friction does negative work when there is motion, but it can also do zero work if there's no motion, making Statement B true. Statement A is false because friction can do positive work in some cases, such as when it helps a car accelerate by providing traction. Correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question:
Read the ${\rm{Statement - A}}$ and ${\rm{Statement - B}}$ carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The coefficient of static friction is the ratio of the maximum static frictional force to the normal force.
Static friction acts to prevent relative motion between surfaces in contact.
explanation: The coefficient of static friction $\left(\mu_{s}\right)$ is indeed the ratio of the maximum static friction to the normal force $\left(\mu_{s}=\dfrac{f_{s \max }}{N}\right)$, and static friction does act to prevent relative motion, as described in Statement B. Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.

question:
Read the ${\rm{Assertion}}$ and ${\rm{Reason}}$ carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: Frictional force is independent of the area of contact between two surfaces.

Reason: Frictional force arises due to intermolecular forces which only depend on the nature of surfaces and not on the area of contact.
explanation: The assertion is a fact about frictional forces, and the reason provided is indeed the correct explanation for why frictional force is independent of the contact area.Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Static Friction on a Horizontal / Vertical Surface

question: A person initially at rest, starts walking towards east without slipping or skidding. What is the type of friction acting on the person from the ground and in which direction the frictional force acts?
explanation:
when we walk, our feet are pushing backward and therefore friction opposes this motion of pushing. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952239/preptracker/questions/q_107606_explanation_1764952238429.png So, the direction of friction is in the direction of to walking $i.e.,$ east direction.
As the person does not skid or slip or roll the friction force which is acting on him is static in nature.
Hence, friction force is static and toward east in nature. So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Static Friction on a Horizontal / Vertical Surface

question: A body of weight $50\,N$ is placed on a horizontal surface as shown in the figure. The minimum force required to move the body is $28.28\,N$. The frictional force and the normal reaction are respectively
explanation:
Given, weight of body, $W=50 N$
Minimum force $=28.28 {~N}$
frictional force,
$f=28.25 \times \cos 45^{\circ}=28.25 \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=20 {~N}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952254/preptracker/questions/q_107604_explanation_1764952253472.png Normal reaction, $R=50-28.2 \sin 45^{\circ}$
$=50-20=30 {~N}$
so,the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Static Friction on a Horizontal / Vertical Surface

question: Two blocks of masses $1\,kg$ and $2\,kg$ connected by a light rod and the system is slipping down a rough incline making an angle $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The frictional coefficient at both the contacts is 0.4 . If the acceleration of the system is $\alpha \sqrt{2}$, the value of $\alpha$ is (use, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
According to the question, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957833/preptracker/questions/q_107599_explanation_1764957832895.png $f_{1}=$ Friction on $1 {~kg}=\mu N_{1}$,
$\Rightarrow f_{1}=0.4 \times \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{2}} N=\dfrac{4}{\sqrt{2}} N$
$f_{2}=$ Friction on $2 {~kg}=\mu N_{2}$,
$f_{2}=0.4 \times \dfrac{20}{\sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{8}{\sqrt{2}} {~N}$
Applying Newton's second law along $X$-axis. Since, both are connected, so assuming common acceleration $=a$
$\therefore\left(\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{2}}+\dfrac{20}{\sqrt{2}}\right)-\left(f_{1}+f_{2}\right)=1 a+2 a$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{30}{\sqrt{2}}-\left(\dfrac{4}{\sqrt{2}}+\dfrac{8}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=3 a$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{30}{\sqrt{2}}-\dfrac{12}{\sqrt{2}}=3 a$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{18}{\sqrt{2}}=3 a \Rightarrow a=\dfrac{6}{\sqrt{2}}$
Since, given in question, $a=\alpha \sqrt{2}$
$\therefore \alpha \sqrt{2}=\dfrac{6}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \alpha=\dfrac{6}{2}=3$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on an Inclined Surface

question: A block is placed on a parabolic shape ramp given by equation, $y=\dfrac{x^{2}}{20}$. If the coefficient of static friction ( $\mu_{s}$ ) is 0.5 , then what is the maximum height above the ground at which the block can be placed without slipping?
explanation:
The given situation is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952252/preptracker/questions/q_107598_explanation_1764952251923.png Let's assume the block is stationary only till point $P$. Slope of point $P=\tan \theta$ (where, $\theta=$ angle of repose)
also: $\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\frac{{{x^2}}}{{20}}} \right) = \frac{x}{{10}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
We know that, the block will remain stationary only up till angle of repose.
We know, angle of repose $\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\mu_{s}\right)$
$ \Rightarrow \theta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}(0.5) \Rightarrow \tan \theta = 0.5{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( 2 \right)$
Now, equating ( $d y / d x$ ) at $P$ with $\tan \theta$ from Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$\dfrac{d y}{d x}=\tan \theta \Rightarrow \dfrac{x}{10}=0.5 \Rightarrow x=5 {~m}$
$\therefore y=\dfrac{x^{2}}{20}=\dfrac{25}{20}=1.25 {~m}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Static Friction on a Curved Surface

question: Block $A$ of mass $3\,kg$ rests on another block $B$ of mass $7\,kg$. The coefficient of friction between $A$ and $B$ is 0.4 , while the coefficient of friction between $B$ and the horizontal floor on which $B$ rests is 0.55. Find the force of friction between $A$ and $B$, when a horizontal force of $50\,N$ is applied on the block. (Use $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
Applied force ( $F$ ) on block $B$ is $50 N$.
Let $f_{B}$ be the maximum friction force between blocks $B$ and, floor. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952249/preptracker/questions/q_107593_explanation_1764952248742.png Now, from block $B$ diagram,
$\,{f_B} = {\mu _B}{N_B}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
where, $N_{B}$ be the normal reaction on block
$=\mu_{B}\left(m_{A}+m_{B}\right) g=0.55(3+7) 10=55 N$
Now, since, $f_{B}>$ applied force $50 N$, the block$B$ will not move.
Hence, $f_{A}=0 N$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Friction in Case of Block on Block

question: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952245/preptracker/questions/q_107592_question_1764952245101.png What is the maximum force $F$ that can be applied on a block ${m_1},$ so that both ${m_1}$ and ${m_2}$ will move together? Assume there is no friction between ${m_1}$ and the horizontal table. The coefficient of friction between ${m_1}$ and ${m_2}$ is $\mu $
explanation:
Given, $F$ is the applied force on mass $m_{1}$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952246/preptracker/questions/q_107592_explanation_1764952245719.png As we know that,
$F=m a \Rightarrow a=\dfrac{F}{m}$
where, $a$ is acceleration of system.
$m=m_{1}+m_{2}$ $i.e.$ mass of system
$\therefore \quad a=\dfrac{F}{m_{1}+m_{2}}$
and since, there should be no relative motion between $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$
$\therefore f=\mu N_{2}=m_{2} a$
where, $f=$ friction force
and $\quad N_{2}=$ normal reaction of mass $m_{2}$.
$\therefore \mu m_{2} g=m_{2} \dfrac{F}{m_{1}+m_{2}}$
$\Rightarrow F=\mu\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) g$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Friction in Case of Block on Block

question: Consider a small block sliding down an inclined plane of inclination $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The coefficient of friction is $\mu=\dfrac{2}{3} x$, where $x$ is the distance (in $m$ ) through which the mass slides down. The distance covered by the mass before it stops is
explanation:
Given, angle of inclination, $\theta=30^{\circ}$.
Coefficient of friction $\mu=\dfrac{2}{3} x$, (where $x$ is distance) and final speed, $v=0$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952209/preptracker/questions/q_107588_explanation_1764952208413.png From given diagram,
Net force, $m a=m g \sin \theta-\mu m g \cos \theta$
$a=g(\sin \theta-\mu \cos \theta)$
where, $a$ is acceleration.
$\therefore a=10\left(\sin 30^{\circ}-\dfrac{2}{3} x \cos 30^{\circ}\right)$
$a=10\left(\dfrac{1}{2}-\dfrac{2}{3} x \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)=10\left(\dfrac{1}{2}-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} x\right)$
We know that,
$\,a = v\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} \Rightarrow vdv = adx\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
where, $v$ is velocity and $d v, d x$ are elemental velocity and displacement, respectively.
Now, on integrating Eq. (1) both sides, we get
$\int_{u=0}^{v=0} v d v=\int_{0}^{x} 10\left(\dfrac{1}{2}-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} x\right) d x$
$\therefore\left(\dfrac{v^{2}}{2}\right)_{0}^{0}=10\left(\dfrac{x}{2}-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \dfrac{x^{2}}{2}\right)_{0}^{x}$
$\Rightarrow 0=\dfrac{10}{2}\left(x-\dfrac{x^{2}}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$
$\Rightarrow x=\dfrac{x^{2}}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{3} m$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on an Inclined Surface

question: A person is managing to be at rest between two vertical walls by pressing one wall by his hands and feet and second wall with his back. The coefficient of friction is 0.5 between his body and the walls. If the force with which the person pushes the wall is $500\,N$, then the mass of the person is (Take, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
The given situation is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952207/preptracker/questions/q_107586_explanation_1764952206836.png Coefficient of friction, $\mu=0.5$, force applied by person on wall, $N=500 N=N_{1}+N_{2}$
Here $N_{3}=N_{1}+N_{2}=500 {~N}$
Let, mass of the person be $m$.
Here, $f_{1}, f_{2}$ and $f_{3}$ be the friction forces and $W$ is weight of man respectively.
As the person have to be at rest $i.e.$ equilibrium
Hence, $f_{1}+f_{2}+f_{3}=m g$
and $f=\mu N$ (where, $N$ is magnitude of normal reaction.)
$\therefore \mu N_{1}+\mu N_{2}+\mu N_{3}=m g$
Since, $N_{1}+N_{2}=N=500 N$
$\therefore \mu N_{1}+\mu N_{2}+\mu\left(N_{1}+N_{2}\right)=m g$
$\Rightarrow \mu N+\mu N=m g$
$\therefore 2 \mu N=m g$
$\Rightarrow 2 \times \dfrac{5}{10} \times 500=m \times 10$
$\Rightarrow m=50 {~kg}$
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Static Friction on a Horizontal / Vertical Surface

question: A block of mass $3\,kg$ is pressed against a vertical wall by applying a force $F$ at an angle $30^{\circ}$ to the horizontal as shown in the figure. As a result, the block is prevented from falling down. If the coefficient of static friction between the block and wall is $\sqrt{3}$, then the value of $F$ is (use, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$)
explanation:
The given situation is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952236/preptracker/questions/q_107579_explanation_1764952234378.png Normal reaction is
$N=F \cos \theta=F \cos 30^{\circ}$
Friction force when block is just on the verge of sliding is
$f=\mu N=\mu F \cos 30^{\circ}=\dfrac{3}{2} F \quad[\therefore \mu=\sqrt{3}]$
As block is in equilibrium,friction $=$ weight of block $(m g)+$ vertical component of force ($F \sin \theta$) $\Rightarrow \dfrac{3}{2} F=3 \times 10+F \times \dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow F=30 N$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on a Horizontal/Vertical Surface

question: A block of mass $m=2 {~kg}$ is initially at rest on a horizontal surface. A horizontal force $\vec{F}_{1}=(6 N) \hat{i}$ and a vertical force $\vec{F}_{2}=(10 N) \hat{j}$ are then applied to the block. The coefficients of static friction and kinetic friction for the block and the surfaces are 0.4 and 0.25, respectively. The magnitude of the frictional force acting on the block is (assume, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
The normal force exerted by the floor on the block is $F_{N}$. Using Newton's second law in vertical directions, we get https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952232/preptracker/questions/q_107577_explanation_1764952232219.png ${F_2} + {F_N} - mg = 0{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Given, $F_{2}=10 {~N}, m=2 {~kg}$ and $g=10 {~ms}^{-2}$
$ \Rightarrow 10 + {F_N} - mg = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
or $F_{N}=(2 \times 10)-10=10 N$
Hence, Limiting static friction, $f_{s, l}=\mu_{s} F_{N}$
$ = 0.4 \times 10 = 4\,N\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
Clearly, it is less than static force $F_{1}(=6 N)$, so the block will move, $i.e.$ we are dealing with kinetic friction now.
Hence, friction force (kinetic) acting on the body is
$f_{k}=\mu_{k} F_{N}=0.25 \times 10=2.5 {~N}$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on a Horizontal/Vertical Surface

question: A block rests on a fixed wedge inclined at an angle $\theta$. The coefficient of friction between the block and plane is $\mu$. The maximum value of $\theta$ for the block to remain motionless on the wedge is
explanation:
The given condition can be shown with a proper FBD of the block, as below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952227/preptracker/questions/q_107574_explanation_1764952226850.png Suppose the mass of the block is $m$ and acceleration due to gravity is $g$.
Normal reaction, $N=m g \cos \theta$
Limiting friction, $\left(f_{s}\right)_{\text {lim }}=\mu_{s} N=\mu m g \cos \theta$
Net driving force, $\left(f_{s}\right)_{\text {net driving }}=m g \sin \theta$
If the block remain motionless on the wedge, then
$\left(f_{s}\right)_{\text {net driving }} \leq\left(f_{s}\right)_{\text {lim }} \Rightarrow m g \sin \theta \leq \mu m g \cos \theta$
$\sin \theta \leq \mu \cos \theta \Rightarrow \tan \theta \leq \mu \Rightarrow \tan \theta_{\text {max }}=\mu$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Static Friction on an Inclined Surface

question: A block of mass $4\,kg$ at rest on a rough inclined plane making an angle of $\theta$ with the horizontal. The coefficient of static friction between the block and plane is 0.5 and the limiting frictional force on the block is $14.14 N$, find the value of $\theta$.
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957815/preptracker/questions/q_107573_explanation_1764957814662.png Given, $m=4 {~kg}, \mu_{s}=0.5$
$f_{s, l}=14.14 {~N}=10 \sqrt{2} {~N}$
Normal reaction force,
$N=m g \cos \theta=4 \times 10 \times \cos \theta=40 \cos \theta$
and limiting friction,
$\Rightarrow f_{s, l}=\mu_{s} N \Rightarrow 10 \sqrt{2}=0.5 \times 40 \cos \theta$
$\Rightarrow 10 \sqrt{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 40 \cos \theta$
$\Rightarrow 10 \sqrt{2}=20 \cos \theta \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\dfrac{10 \sqrt{2}}{20}$
$\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \theta=45^{\circ}$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on an Inclined Surface

question: Two touching blocks 1 and 2 are placed on an inclined plane forming an angle $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The masses are $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ and the coefficient of friction between the inclined plane and the two blocks are $1.5 \mu$ and $1.0 \mu$, respectively. The force of reaction between the blocks during the motion is ($g=$ acceleration due to gravity)
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957808/preptracker/questions/q_107569_explanation_1764957807965.png Coefficient of friction between the surface of plane and surface of first block, $\mu_{1}=1.5 \mu$ Coefficient of friction between the surface of plane and surface of second block, $\mu_{2}=1 \mu$
Now, free body diagram for the first block, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957809/preptracker/questions/q_107569_explanation_1764957808577.png If $a$ be the common acceleration, then
$m_{1} g \sin 60^{\circ}-\mu_{1} \cdot R_{1}+R=m_{1} a$
$m_{1} g \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-1.5 \mu m_{1} g \cos 60^{\circ}+R=m_{1} a$
${m_1}g\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} - \frac{{1.5\mu {m_1}g}}{2} + R = {m_1}a\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Similarly, for second body, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957810/preptracker/questions/q_107569_explanation_1764957809443.png $m_{2} g \sin 60^{\circ}-\mu_{2} R_{2}-R=m_{2} a$
$m_{2} g \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\mu m_{2} g \cos 60^{\circ}-R=m_{2} a$
${m_2}g\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} - \frac{{\mu {m_2}g}}{2} - R = {m_2}a\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$\dfrac{m_{1} g \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\dfrac{1.5 \mu m_{1} g}{2}+R}{m_{2} g \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\dfrac{\mu m_{2} g}{2}-R}=\dfrac{m_{1} a}{m_{2} a}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} m_{1} m_{2} g-0.75 \mu m_{1} m_{2} g+m_{2} R$
$=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} m_{1} m_{2} g-0.5 \mu m_{1} m_{2} g-m_{1} R$
$\Rightarrow\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) R=0.25 \mu m_{1} m_{2} g$
$\Rightarrow R=\dfrac{\mu m_{1} m_{2} g}{4\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right)}$
Hence, the force of reaction between the blocks doing the motion is,
$R = \frac{1}{4}\left( {\frac{{{m_1}{m_2}}}{{\left( {{m_1} + {m_2}} \right)}}} \right)\mu g$.
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on an Inclined Surface

question: Consider a system of two masses and a pulley shown in the figure. The coefficient of friction between the two blocks and also between block and table is 0.1. Find the force $F$, that must be given to the $0.8\,kg$ block such that it attains an acceleration of $5 {~m} / {s}^{2}$. (Assume, acceleration due to gravity, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$.)
explanation:
Given, acceleration of block, $a=5 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ and coefficient of friction between two blocks and table, $\mu=0.1$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952212/preptracker/questions/q_107567_explanation_1764952211897.png Free body diagram for $0.8\,kg$ block. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952213/preptracker/questions/q_107567_explanation_1764952212538.png $\therefore$ Equation of motion for $0.8\,kg$ block,
$F-T-\mu R-\mu R_{1}=0.8 a$
$\Rightarrow F-T-0.1(0.8+0.2) g-0.1 \times 0.2 g=0.8 a$
$F-T-0.1 \times 10-0.1 \times 0.2 \times 10=0.8 \times 5$
$F - T = 5.2\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Free body diagram for $0.2\,kg$ body, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952216/preptracker/questions/q_107567_explanation_1764952215910.png So, $T-\mu R_{1}=0.2 a$
$T-0.1 \times 0.2 g=0.2 \times 5$
$T-0.02 \times 10=1.0$
$T = 1.2\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$F-1.2=5.2$
$F=5.2+1.2=6.4 {~N}$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on a Horizontal/Vertical Surface

question: A block of mass $10\,kg$, initially at rest, makes a downward motion on $45^{\circ}$ inclined plane. Then the distance travelled by the block after $2 s$ is (Assume the coefficient of kinetic friction to be 0.3 and $g=10 {~ms}^{-2}$ )
explanation:
The figure given below, shows the downward motion of a body on the inclined plane, then https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955484/preptracker/questions/q_107563_explanation_1764955484240.png The equation of acceleration in downwards motion of the block, $a=F_{\text {net }} / m$
$ \Rightarrow a = g\sin \theta - \mu g\cos \theta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Given, $m=10 {~kg}, \theta=45^{\circ}, \mu=0.3$ and time, $t=2 s$,
Putting the given values in Eq. (1), we get
$\Rightarrow a=g\left(\sin 45^{\circ}-0.3 \cos 45^{\circ}\right)$
$=\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{2}}(1-0.3)=\dfrac{10 \times 0.7}{\sqrt{2}} {~m} / {s}^{2}$
Displacement of the block, from the second equation of the motion,
$s=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2} \quad(\because u=0)$
$s=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{10 \times 0.7}{\sqrt{2}} \times(2)^{2}=\dfrac{7 \times 4}{2 \sqrt{2}}=7 \sqrt{2} {~m}$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on an Inclined Surface

question: A small block starts sliding down an inclined plane forming an angle $45^{\circ}$ horizontal. The coefficient of friction $\mu$, varies with distance $s$ as $\mu=c s^{2}$ where, $c$ is a constant of appropriate dimensions, then distance covered by the block before it stops is
explanation:
Given that, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952201/preptracker/questions/q_107558_explanation_1764952201212.png $\mu=c s^{2} \quad\{\because s=$ distance and $c={constant}\}$
Net force on the block is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952203/preptracker/questions/q_107558_explanation_1764952202032.png $M g \sin \theta-f=M a$
$\Rightarrow M g \sin \theta-\mu M g \cos \theta=M a$
$g \sin \theta-\mu g \cos \theta=a$
$g\left[ {\sin \theta - c{s^2}\cos \theta } \right] = a\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$\because \quad a = \frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = v\frac{{dv}}{{ds}} \Rightarrow ads = vdv\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$\Rightarrow g\left[\sin \theta-c s^{2} \cos \theta\right] d s=v d v$
Integrating both sides, we get
$ \Rightarrow (g\sin \theta )s - g\frac{{c{s^3}}}{3}\cos \theta = \frac{{{v^2}}}{2} + K{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
For $\theta=45^{\circ}$, we have,
$\left(\dfrac{g}{\sqrt{2}}\right) s-\left(\dfrac{c s^{3}}{3}\right) \dfrac{g}{\sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{v^{2}}{2}+K$
Initially $t=0, s=0, v=0$, substituting these,
we get $K=0$
so, $\dfrac{g}{\sqrt{2}}\left(s-\dfrac{c s^{3}}{3}\right)=\dfrac{v^{2}}{2} \Rightarrow s-\dfrac{c s^{3}}{3}=\dfrac{v^{2}}{2} \times \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{g}$
The body stops, when $v=0 \Rightarrow s-\dfrac{c s^{3}}{3}=0$
$\Rightarrow s=\dfrac{c s^{3}}{3} \Rightarrow s=\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{c}}$
So,the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on an Inclined Surface

question: A movable steel plate is placed between fixed steel and brass plates and the stack of plates is subjected to a weight of $100\,N$ as shown in the figure. The coefficient of kinetic friction for steel on steel is 0.57 and for steel on brass is 0.44. Assuming that the entire weight comes onto the stack and that the weight of the plates is negligible in comparison to the applied weight, the force required to move the middle plate (in $N$ ) is
explanation:
For given figure, $\mu_{s s}=$ coefficient of friction between steel and steel $=0.57$ and $\mu_{s B}=$ coefficient of friction between steel and brass $=0.44$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952198/preptracker/questions/q_107557_explanation_1764952198246.png Free body diagram https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952199/preptracker/questions/q_107557_explanation_1764952199136.png where, $f_{s s}=$ friction between steel plates and $f_{S B}=$ friction between steel and brass plates. $\Rightarrow$ For kinetic-function, the applied force (F) is equal or greater than friction force for moving the steel plate, so $f_{S S}+f_{S B} \leq F$
Now, $f_{s s}=\mu_{s s} \times R=0.57 \times 100=57 {~N}$
$f_{S B}=\mu_{S B} \times R=0.44 \times 100=44 {~N}$
$\therefore F=(57+44)=N$
$=101$ newton $N$
So,the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on a Horizontal/Vertical Surface

question: A block of mass $5\,kg$ is pulled by a force $F$ as shown in the figure. If the coefficient of friction is 0.1, then the force needed to accelerate the block to $3 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ to the right is close to
explanation:
Component of force accelerating the block is $F \cos \theta$.
Net force on block, $F_{\text {net }}=F \cos \theta-\mu N$
Now, normal reaction on the block,
$N=m g-F \sin \theta$ )$\therefore F_{\text {net }}=F \cos \theta-\mu(m g-F \sin \theta)$
$=F(\cos \theta-\mu \sin \theta)-\mu m g$
$=F\left(\cos 30^{\circ}-0.1 \times \sin 30^{\circ}\right)-0.1 \times 5 \times 9.8 {~N}$
$\Rightarrow F_{\text {net }}=(0.816 \times F-4.9) N$
From, $F_{\text {net }}=m a$, we have,
$0.816F - 4.9 = 5 \times 3$
$ \Rightarrow F = \frac{{19.9}}{{0.816}} = 24.38N \simeq 24N$
So,the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on a Horizontal/Vertical Surface

question: There are two inclined surfaces of equal length $(L)$ and same angle of inclination $45^\circ $ with the horizontal. One of them is rough and the other is perfectly smooth. A given body takes 2 times as much time to slide down on rough surface than on the smooth surface. The coefficient of kinetic friction ( $\mu_{k}$ ) between the object and the rough surface is close to
explanation:
$t_{\text {rough }}=2 t_{\text {smooth }}$
$a_{\text {smooth }}=g \sin \theta$
For a given length of the plane
$t \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \Rightarrow t_{\text {smooth }} \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{g \sin \theta}}$
$a_{\text {rough }}=g \sin \theta-\mu_{k} g \cos \theta$
$\dfrac{t_{\text {rough }}}{t_{\text {smooth }}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{\sin \theta}}{\sqrt{\sin \theta-\mu_{k} \cos \theta}}=2$
Squaring both sides
$\dfrac{\sin \theta}{\sin \theta-\mu_{k} \cos \theta}=4 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}{\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}-\mu_{k} \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}=4$
$1-\mu_{k}=\dfrac{1}{4} \Rightarrow \mu_{k}=\dfrac{3}{4}=0.75$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on an Inclined Surface

question: A block of certain mass is placed on a rough floor. The coefficients of static and kinetic friction between the block and the floor are 0.4 and 0.25 respectively. A constant horizontal force \[F = 20\;N\] acts on it so that the velocity of the block varies with time according to the following graph. The mass of the block is nearly (Take \[g \simeq 10\;m{s^{ - 2}}\])
explanation:
By work $ - $ energy theorem,
$W=\Delta K=K_{f}-K_{i}$
$F_{n e t} \cdot x=\dfrac{1}{2} m\left(v_{f}^{2}-v_{i}^{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow\left(20-\mu_{k} m g\right) \times$ area under $v-t$ graph.
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m\left(20^{2}-0\right)$
$\Rightarrow(20-0.25 m \times 10) \times\left(\dfrac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 20\right)=\dfrac{1}{2} m(20)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 600-75 m=200 m \Rightarrow 600=275 m$
$ \Rightarrow m = 2.18\,kg$
So, correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on a Horizontal/Vertical Surface

question: A wooden block of mass $M$ lies on a rough floor. Another wooden block of the same mass is hanging from the point $O$ through strings as shown in the figure. To achieve equilibrium, the co-efficient of static friction between the block and the floor is
explanation:
Free body diagrams of the block on the table and joint $O$ are shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952197/preptracker/questions/q_107552_explanation_1764952196790.png ${f_l} = {\text{Limiting}}\,\,\,\,{\text{friction}} = \mu N = \mu Mg\,\,\left( {\because \,N = Mg} \right)$
Now, $T' = T\cos \theta = {f_l} = \mu Mg$
and $T\sin \theta = Mg$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,\cot \theta = \mu $
subject: Physics
subtopic: Static Friction on a Horizontal / Vertical Surface

question: A block of mass  $5\,kg$  is placed on a rough inclined surface as shown in the figure. If $\vec{F}_{1}$ is the force required to just move the block up the inclined plane and $\vec{F}_{2}$ is the force required to just prevent the block from sliding down, then the value of $\left|\vec{F}_{1}\right|-\left|\vec{F}_{2}\right|$ is [Use $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$ ]
explanation:
Given : $\mu=0.1$,
Mass, $m = 5\,kg$
Let $F_{1}$ be the force that is required to just move the block up inclined plane.
${F_1} = mg\sin \theta + \mu \,mg\cos \theta $
$ = 5 \times 10\sin 30^\circ + 0.1 \times 5 \times 10 \times \cos 30^\circ $
$ = 25 + 2.5\sqrt 3 N\quad \quad (1)$
Let $F_{2}$ be the force that prevents block from sliding down,
$\begin{align*}& F_{2}=m g \sin \theta-\mu m g \cos \theta \\& =5 \times 10 \sin 30^{\circ}-0.1 \times 5 \times 10 \cos 30^{\circ} \\& =25-2.5 \sqrt{3} \tag{2}\end{align*}$
From equation (1) and (2), we get,
$\left|\vec{F}_{1}\right|-\left|\vec{F}_{2}\right|=5 \sqrt{3} N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Static Friction on an Inclined Surface

question: In the given arrangement of a doubly inclined plane two blocks of masses $M$ and $m$ are placed. The blocks are connected by a light string passing over an ideal pulley as shown. The coefficient of friction between the surface  of the plane and the blocks is 0.25 . The value of $m$ , for which $M = 10\,kg$ will move down with an acceleration of  $2\,m/{s^2}$  is (Take $g = 10\,m/{s^2}{\rm{ }}$ and $\tan \,\,37^\circ  = \frac{3}{4}$ )
explanation:
By free body diagrams for block $M$ and $m$ are shown in figure (1) and (2).
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956843/preptracker/questions/q_35425_explanation_1764956842931.png
From figure (1),
$Ma = Mg\,\,\sin 53^\circ - {f_1} - T$
$Ma = Mg\,\,\sin 53^\circ - \mu Mg\cos 53^\circ - T$ $\quad \quad (1)$
From figure (2),
$m a=T-m g \sin 37^{\circ}-\mu m g \cos 37^{\circ}$
or $T=m a+m g \sin 37^{\circ}+\mu m g \cos 37^{\circ}(2)$
Putting eqn. (2) in (1) and substituting the given values, we get,
$\begin{aligned}& 10 a=M g \dfrac{4}{5}-(0.25)(10)(10)\left(\dfrac{3}{5}\right) \\& -m a-m(10)\left(\dfrac{3}{5}\right)\end{aligned}$
$-(0.25) m(10)\left(\dfrac{4}{5}\right)$
$\therefore \quad 10 \times 2=10 \times 10 \times \dfrac{4}{5}-15-2 m-6 m-2 m$
or $20 - 80 + 15 = - 10m \Rightarrow m = 4.5\,kg$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on an Inclined Surface

question:
Consider a block and trolley system as shown in figure. If the coefficient of kinetic friction between the trolley and the surface is 0.04 , the acceleration of the system in $m{s^{ - 2}}$ is

(Consider that the string is massless and unstretchable and the pulley is also massless and frictionless)
explanation:
$\mu = 0.04,\,\,{m_1} = 20\,kg,\,\,{m_2} = 6\,kg$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951469/preptracker/questions/q_35421_explanation_1764951469465.png
$f_{k}=\mu m_{2} g$
$=0.04 \times 20 \times 10=8 N$
$a=\dfrac{\text { Net force along } \vec{a}}{\text { total mass }}=\dfrac{6 g-f_{k}}{20+6}$

$\Rightarrow a=\dfrac{6 \times 10-8}{26}$
$ \Rightarrow a = 2\,m/{s^2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on a Horizontal/Vertical Surface

question: A given object takes $n$ times the time to slide down $45^{\circ}$ rough inclined plane as it takes the time to slide down an identical perfectly smooth $45^{\circ}$ inclined plane. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the object and the surface of inclined plane is
explanation:
For smooth surface
$a_{1}=g \sin \theta$ and time, $T=t$
For rough surface
$a_{2}=g \sin \theta-m g \cos \theta$ and time, $T=n t$
$S_{1}=S_{2}$
$\therefore \dfrac{1}{2} a_{1} t_{1}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} a_{2} t_{2}^{2}(\because u=0)$
$\Rightarrow g \sin \theta t^{2}=(g \sin \theta-\mu g \cos \theta) n^{2} t^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \sin \theta=(\sin \theta-\mu \cos \theta) n^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\tan \theta}{n^{2}}=\tan \theta-\mu$
$\Rightarrow \mu_{k}=\left(1-\dfrac{1}{n^{2}}\right) \tan \theta$
$\therefore \mu_{k}=1-\dfrac{1}{n^{2}}\left(\because \theta=45^{\circ}\right)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Kinetic Friction on an Inclined Surface

question: A heavy box of mass  $50\,kg$  is moving on a horizontal surface. If co-efficient of kinetic  friction between the box and horizontal surface is 0.3 , then force of kinetic friction is
subject: Physics

question: A  $2\,kg$  brick begins to slide over a surface which is inclined at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with respect to horizontal axis. The co-efficient of static friction between their surfaces is
explanation:
If ' $\alpha$ ' is angle of repose then
$\tan \alpha=\mu_{s}$
$\therefore \mu_{s}=\tan 45^{\circ}=1$
